1:
"Then said the high priest, Are these things so?" The witnesses note the high priest's question and the character of the great speech it occasions. Ellicott surveys the discourse: "It is clearly an unfinished fragment, interrupted by the clamours of the by-standers (Acts 7:51) — the torso, as it were, of a great apologia. Its very incompleteness... [is an] indirect proof that we have a true, though not necessarily a verbatim, report." He notes the probable source: "looking to the relations between St. Luke and St. Paul, and to the prominence of the latter among the accusers of Stephen, there is a strong probability that the report was derived from him."

Ellicott further characterizes the speech as "the first great survey of the history of Israel as a process of divine education," containing "the germs which were... afterwards developed by St. Paul... and by Apollos." He also notes it brings together "a considerable number of real or seeming inaccuracies in the details of the history," which form "something like a crucial test of theories which men have formed as to the nature and limits of inspiration."

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley gives the speech's design: Stephen, accused of blasphemy against Moses, the temple, and the law, answers by rehearsing Israel's history to show that "the promise [is] more ancient than the law... God never confined his presence to this one place or to the observers of the law... But you and your fathers have always been evil... resisted God and his Spirit, killed the prophets and the Messiah." The history becomes an indictment.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which opens the speech. The witnesses agree: the high priest's question "Are these things so?" gives Stephen the opening for his great defense — an unfinished apologia (interrupted at v.51), probably reported by Paul who heard it (Ellicott), surveying Israel's history as a process of divine education. The speech, far from a direct denial of the charges, traces God's dealings with the patriarchs to show that the promise preceded the law, that God's presence was never confined to the temple, and that Israel had always rejected God's messengers (Wesley) — building toward the charge that they had now killed the Messiah himself. The verse also opens the famous series of historical and chronological cruxes that test theories of inspiration (Ellicott). The verse sets the stage for the longest speech in Acts: Stephen's masterly survey of sacred history, which both answers the charge of blasphemy (by his evident reverence) and turns it back upon his accusers (by showing their fathers' rebellion). The proto-martyr begins his defense and his indictment.
2:
"And he said, Men, brethren, and fathers, hearken; The God of glory appeared unto our father Abraham, when he was in Mesopotamia, before he dwelt in Charran." The witnesses note Stephen's respectful address and his opening with God's appearance to Abraham. On the address, Barnes: "These were the usual titles by which the sanhedrim was addressed. In all this Stephen was perfectly respectful." Clarke notes it is really two classes: "the elders and scribes, whom he addressed as fathers; and the common people, whom he calls brethren."

On "the God of glory," the witnesses note its refutation of the blasphemy charge. Robertson: "The God characterized by glory... as seen in the Shekinah, the visible radiance of God... By these words Stephen refutes the charge of blasphemy against God." Calvin: he "calleth him the God of glory, that he may distinguish him from the false and feigned gods, who alone is worthy of glory."

On "before he dwelt in Charran," the witnesses address the first apparent discrepancy. Ellicott: "Here the call of Abraham is spoken of as before he sojourned in Haran... In Genesis 12:1 it is first mentioned after Abraham's removal thither. On the other hand, Genesis 15:7 speaks of God as bringing him from Ur of the Chaldees... and this is confirmed by Joshua 24:3, Nehemiah 9:7." Stephen, like Philo and Josephus, stresses the first call as well as the second; "it cannot be said that the statement is at variance with the Old Testament narrative."

The witnesses agree: Stephen opens respectfully ("Men, brethren, and fathers"), addressing the council with due honour (Barnes, Calvin), and begins with the appearance of "the God of glory" to Abraham — a phrase invoking the Shekinah and refuting the charge of blaspheming God (Robertson, Calvin). On the first apparent discrepancy — that God called Abraham "before he dwelt in Charran," whereas Genesis 12:1 mentions the call after his removal there — the witnesses resolve it: Genesis 15:7, Joshua 24:3, and Nehemiah 9:7 confirm the earlier call from Ur, and Stephen, like Philo and Josephus, rightly stresses both calls (Ellicott, Calvin). The verse displays Stephen's reverence (answering the blasphemy charge) and his theological point: God appeared to Abraham in a strange land, before temple or law, showing that the divine presence is not confined to one place — the thread that runs through the whole speech. The God of glory, who called Abraham in Mesopotamia, is not bound to the temple in Jerusalem.
3:
"And said unto him, Get thee out of thy country, and from thy kindred, and come into the land which I shall shew thee." The witnesses note the call of Abraham and the faith it required. Barnes draws out the faith: "to leave his country and home, to go into a land of strangers, not knowing whither he went, required strong confidence in God... He went, therefore, trusting simply to the promise of God. And thus his conduct illustrated precisely what we are to do in all the future — we are to trust simply to the promise of God, and do that which he requires. This is faith." He cites Hebrews 11:8-9.

The grammatical voices note the indefinite promise. Robertson: "Which I shall shew thee... Abraham followed on as God led him." The land was not named; Abraham went not knowing where, on the bare word of God.

The Reformed Calvin draws out the trial of faith: "the obedience of Abraham was so much the more to be commended, because the sweetness of his native soil keepeth him not back from going willingly, as it were, into exile; and in that he doubteth not to follow God, although there appear no certain resting-place." He applies it to all believers: "We are not all simply commanded to forsake our country, but we are commanded to deny ourselves... if father and mother, wife and children, hinder us from following God, we must forsake them all... Let us have the example of Abraham always before our eyes. He is the father of the faithful."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: God's call to Abraham — to leave country and kindred for an unnamed land — required and displayed strong faith: trusting simply the promise of God, going not knowing where (Barnes, Robertson, Hebrews 11:8). Calvin draws the trial and the application: Abraham's obedience, leaving his native soil for uncertain exile on the bare word of God, is the pattern of all faith; and believers, though not all called to leave their country, are called to deny themselves and forsake all that hinders them from following God. The verse displays the faith of Abraham, the father of the faithful, who obeyed where he could not see — the model of the believer's trust in God's promise. Stephen, by recounting this, both honours the patriarch (answering the charge of dishonouring the fathers) and presses his theological point: God's dealings began with a call out of the land, before any settled possession — the divine purpose not bound to place. To follow God by faith, where he leads and as he commands, is the calling of every believer after the example of Abraham.
4:
"Then came he out of the land of the Chaldaeans, and dwelt in Charran: and from thence, when his father was dead, he removed him into this land, wherein ye now dwell." The witnesses note the readiness of Abraham's faith and a famous chronological crux. On the obedience, Calvin: "when he is called he maketh no delay, but maketh haste, and subdueth all his affections, that they may obey the holy commandment of God."

On "when his father was dead," a genuine difficulty arises. Barnes states it: "From Genesis 11:26, it would seem that Abraham was born when Terah was seventy... From Genesis 12:4, Abraham was seventy-five when he departed from Haran... Yet in Genesis 11:32, Terah was two hundred and five years old when he died; thus leaving sixty years of Terah's life beyond the time when Abraham left Haran." The witnesses propose solutions: (1) the Samaritan reading (Terah died at 105); (2) Abraham was not the eldest son, named first for prominence as Shem was, so born when Terah was 130 (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson); (3) the Jewish view that Terah's relapse into idolatry was a "moral death" before Abraham left (Barnes, Kuinoel).

Ellicott prefers a broader explanation: "the memory of St. Stephen or of his reporter dwelt upon the broad outlines of the history, and was indifferent to chronological details. It is remarkable that like difficulties present themselves in St. Paul's own survey... A man speaking for his life... does not commonly carry with him a memoria technica of chronological minutiae."

The witnesses agree: Abraham, called, obeyed without delay (Calvin), came to Haran, and after his father's death removed to Canaan. The verse presents a famous chronological crux: Terah died at 205, yet Abraham (born, per the surface reading, when Terah was 70) left Haran at 75, leaving 60 years unaccounted. The witnesses resolve it variously: the Samaritan reading (Terah at 105); Abraham not the eldest, named first for prominence and born when Terah was 130 (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson); the Jewish "moral death" of Terah's idolatry (Barnes); or Stephen's indifference to chronological minutiae in a speech given from memory (Ellicott). The verse displays both the promptness of Abraham's faith and one of the cruxes that test theories of inspiration — Stephen, like Paul, surveying the broad outlines of history without pedantic chronology. The difficulty, the witnesses note, does not impugn the truth of the narrative or Luke's faithful report, but illustrates the freedom with which the broad sweep of sacred history was recounted.
5:
"And he gave him none inheritance in it, no, not so much as to set his foot on: yet he promised that he would give it to him for a possession, and to his seed after him, when as yet he had no child." The witnesses note that Abraham received no possession but the promise, the trial of his faith. Barnes: "Abraham led a wandering life; and this passage means that he did not himself receive a permanent possession or residence in that land. The only land which he owned was the field which he purchased of the children of Heth, for a burial-place." That was bought, not given. The promise was for his seed.

On "when as yet he had no child," the witnesses note the strength of the faith. Robertson: "He had only the promise of God about the land and the child." Barnes: "This is mentioned as a strong instance of his faith: Who against hope believed in hope (Romans 4:18)." The promise of a land for his seed, when he had no child and his wife was barren, was a notable trial.

The Reformed Calvin draws out three things: that God exercised Abraham's patience (he possessed not a foot's breadth save the burial-field he bought); that God upheld him by his word (the promise his only stay); and that the promise was such as "did not much differ from a mere mock" — promised to his seed when he was old and his wife barren. "This was a notable trial of faith, in that Abraham, without asking any question, did obediently and meekly embrace that which he had heard proceed out of the mouth of the Lord."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Abraham received no inheritance in the land — not a foot's breadth, save the burial-field he bought (Barnes) — but only the promise that his seed would possess it, given when he had no child and his wife was barren. The promise, resting on the bare word of God against all natural hope (Romans 4:18, Barnes), was a notable trial of faith: God exercised Abraham's patience, upheld him by his word alone, and gave a promise that seemed almost a mockery, yet which Abraham meekly embraced (Calvin). The verse displays the faith that rests on the promise where it sees nothing of the fulfillment — and presses Stephen's point against the Jews who presumed on their possession of the land: even Abraham held it only by promise, as a stranger. The believer, like Abraham, is heir to promises he does not yet possess, and must rest on God's word against sight. The land was promised, not given; the seed was promised, though there was no child — and Abraham believed.
6:
"And God spake on this wise, That his seed should sojourn in a strange land; and that they should bring them into bondage, and entreat them evil four hundred years." The witnesses note the prophecy of the Egyptian bondage and a chronological crux. Chrysostom marks the providence: "See, what a number of years the Promise has been given... He here shows, how God Himself suffered them to be afflicted... So also now, though He has promised a Kingdom, yet He suffers us to be exercised in temptations."

On "four hundred years," a famous crux arises. Barnes: "In Exodus 12:40, it is said that their sojourning in Egypt was four hundred and thirty years... Paul (Galatians 3:17) says it was four hundred and thirty years from the promise given to Abraham to the law on Sinai." The witnesses resolve it: the 400 is a round number for 430 (Clarke, Barnes); and the 430 (per the LXX and Samaritan reading of Exodus 12:40, "in Canaan and in Egypt") covers the whole period from Abraham's call, not Egypt alone — the actual time in Egypt being about 215 years (Ellicott, Robertson). Barnes: "the four hundred and thirty years includes the whole time from the calling of Abraham to the departure from Egypt."

The Reformed Calvin draws the trial: "it must needs be that the mind of the godly man was sore wounded, when he heard that his seed should serve, and be villanously and cruelly entreated... God dealeth oftentimes with us thus, so that he may seem contrary to himself... the purpose of God [is] to extend the sight of our faith the farther, to show his promises afar off."

The witnesses agree: God foretold to Abraham that his seed would sojourn in a strange land and suffer bondage four hundred years — a prophecy that both displayed God's foreknowledge and tried Abraham's faith (the promise of the land set against the promise of bondage). On the chronological crux (400 vs 430 years, vs the ~215 actually in Egypt), the witnesses resolve it: 400 a round number for 430, which (per the LXX) covers the whole period from Abraham's call, not Egypt alone (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson). Calvin draws the lesson: God seems contrary to himself, promising the land yet foretelling bondage, to extend the reach of faith and show his promises afar off. Chrysostom adds the providence: God suffered the affliction, as he suffers his people to be exercised in temptation though he has promised the kingdom. The verse displays God's sovereign foreknowledge and the trial of faith: the bondage foretold, the deliverance certain, the believer called to trust the promise through long affliction.
7:
"And the nation to whom they shall be in bondage will I judge, said God: and after that shall they come forth, and serve me in this place." The witnesses note God's promise to judge Egypt and to bring Israel forth to serve him. On "will I judge," Barnes: "The word judge often means to execute judgment, as well as to pronounce it; that is, to punish... God regarded their oppressive acts as deserving his indignation, and he evinced it in the plagues... and in their overthrow in the Red Sea." Clarke: "Will I punish."

On "serve me in this place," the witnesses note the combination of texts. Ellicott: "The verse combines the promise to Abraham in Genesis 15:17 with a free rendering of the sign given to Moses (Exodus 3:12), which referred not to Canaan but to Horeb." Robertson: "Quoted from Exodus 3:12 and referring to Sinai or Horeb, but Stephen applies it to the Promised Land." Stephen freely combines the promises, applying the worship to the land of promise.

The Reformed Calvin draws out God's care for his church: "whereas God doth punish the cruelty and tyranny of the wicked Egyptians, he doth that for his people's sake... so often as we are unjustly afflicted by the wicked, let us remember that God is the Judge of the world, who will let no injuries be unpunished." And on the end of deliverance: "God chose both a peculiar people and a peculiar place for the true worship of his name... God's benefits must be referred to this end, that men might be brought to addict and give over themselves wholly to him."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: God promised to judge (punish) the oppressing nation and to bring Israel forth to serve him — a promise combining the word to Abraham (Genesis 15) with the sign to Moses (Exodus 3:12), freely applied by Stephen to the land of promise (Ellicott, Robertson). The "judge" displays God's vindication of his afflicted people: he is the Judge of the world who lets no injury go unpunished (Calvin), and who delivers his church for his own glory. The end of the deliverance, Calvin notes, is the true worship of God — that the redeemed should give themselves wholly to him. The verse displays both God's justice (punishing the oppressor) and his purpose (a people delivered to serve him), and presses Stephen's theme: the worship of God, the goal of redemption, was promised before the temple was built — the divine presence and service not bound to place. God judges the oppressor and redeems his people, that they may serve him; and this purpose preceded all the institutions on which the Jews presumed.
8:
"And he gave him the covenant of circumcision: and so Abraham begat Isaac, and circumcised him the eighth day; and Isaac begat Jacob; and Jacob begat the twelve patriarchs." The witnesses note the covenant of circumcision and its place in the argument. Ellicott draws out the apologetic point: "Here we trace an indirect reference to the charge that he had spoken against the customs... He does assert (1) that the covenant of circumcision followed on the promise to Abraham, and therefore was not the ground of his election, and so lays the foundation for St. Paul's argument in Galatians 3:17; (2) that, though part of a provisional, not of a permanent, system, it came from God's appointment, and therefore was to be spoken of with all reverence."

The grammatical voices note the meaning of "covenant." Robertson: "A covenant marked by circumcision... of which circumcision is the sign (Romans 4:11)." The covenant was the promise; circumcision the sign and seal that followed it.

The Reformed Calvin draws out the order of grace: "if Abraham was called, and the land and redemption promised to his seed before such time as he was circumcised, it appeareth that the glory of the whole stock doth not depend upon circumcision. Paul useth the same argument in Romans 4." He notes the sacramental lesson: "God maketh his covenant with us in the sacraments, that he may declare his love toward us... they are no vain figures; because God, who is true, figureth nothing there which he doth not perform."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God gave Abraham the covenant of circumcision (the sign and seal following the promise, Robertson), and so the line ran from Isaac through Jacob to the twelve patriarchs. Stephen's point is apologetic: circumcision followed the promise and was not the ground of Abraham's election (laying the foundation for Paul's argument in Galatians 3:17, Romans 4), yet came from God and is to be reverenced — clearing him of the charge of speaking against the customs (Ellicott, Calvin). The verse displays both the reverence Stephen shows for the divine institution (answering the blasphemy charge) and the theological point that the covenant of grace preceded its sign: the Jews' boasting in circumcision rests on a sign that came after the promise. Calvin draws the sacramental lesson: the sacraments are God's true seals of his love, figuring nothing he does not perform. The verse marks the divine covenant and its sign, and presses the order of grace: promise first, circumcision after — the seal, not the ground, of God's electing love.
9:
"And the patriarchs, moved with envy, sold Joseph into Egypt: but God was with him," The witnesses note the patriarchs' crime against Joseph — and its typological significance. Ellicott draws out the key thread: "This, interpreted by what follows, is the first step in the long induction which is to show that the elect of God had always been opposed and rejected by those who were for the time the representatives of the nation. Envy had actuated the patriarchs when they sold Joseph; envy had led their descendants to deliver up Jesus (Matthew 27:18). But man's evil will had not frustrated God's gracious purpose."

Chrysostom (under v.6) marks the type: "the type of Christ. Though they had no fault to find with him, and though he came on purpose to bring them their food, they thus ill-treated him." The rejection of Joseph by his brethren prefigures the rejection of Christ by his own.

The Reformed Calvin draws out the shame of the nation: "all the patriarchs, Benjamin excepted, had polluted themselves with that treachery; therefore in that Stephen vouchsafeth to give them an honorable name [patriarchs] that redoundeth to the greater reproach of the nation. They boasted proudly of their fathers; he showeth what manner of persons the chief of them were; to wit, murderers of their brother." And on "God was with him": "God was bountiful and merciful to those which were, as it were, unwilling... Joseph was not delivered because he had called upon God in the temple, but afar off in Egypt."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the patriarchs, moved with envy, sold Joseph into Egypt — yet God was with him. This is the first step in Stephen's great induction (Ellicott): that God's chosen are always rejected by the nation's representatives, as envy led the patriarchs to sell Joseph and their descendants to deliver up Jesus. The rejection of Joseph prefigures the rejection of Christ (Chrysostom); and the honourable name "patriarchs" only deepens the reproach — the boasted fathers were the murderers of their brother (Calvin). Yet "God was with him": man's evil will did not frustrate God's purpose, and Joseph was delivered far from the temple, in Egypt — pressing Stephen's theme that God's presence is not bound to place. The verse displays both the typology (Joseph the type of the rejected Christ) and the providence (God overruling the crime for good). Stephen turns the history into an indictment: the fathers in whom they boasted rejected God's chosen, as they now reject the Messiah — and God was with the rejected one still.
10:
"And delivered him out of all his afflictions, and gave him favour and wisdom in the sight of Pharaoh king of Egypt; and he made him governor over Egypt and all his house." The witnesses note God's deliverance and exaltation of the rejected Joseph. Barnes: "restored him to liberty from his servitude and humiliation, and raised him up to high honours and offices in Egypt. Favour and wisdom — the favour was the result of his wisdom... particularly evinced in interpreting the dreams of Pharaoh."

The grammatical voices note the title. Robertson: "Pharaoh is not a name, but a title, the Egyptian meaning great house." Joseph, the slave and captive, was raised to govern Egypt and all Pharaoh's house — a complete reversal of his humiliation.

The Reformed Calvin draws out God's providence in the favour: "God could have delivered him by some other means, but his counsel had respect unto a farther thing, that Joseph, being ruler of the kingdom, might entertain his father and all his family... although Joseph be made chief ruler of Egypt by Pharaoh, yet is he lifted up to so great honour properly by the hand of God." He notes that all human aptness and gifts are God's: "what aptness and readiness soever is in men, it ought to be reckoned amongst the gifts of God."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: God delivered the rejected Joseph from all his afflictions, gave him favour and wisdom before Pharaoh, and made him governor over Egypt — a complete reversal of his humiliation (Barnes), the slave raised to rule the "great house" of Pharaoh (Robertson). Calvin draws out the providence: God exalted Joseph not merely for his own sake but that he might preserve his father's family; and though Pharaoh made him ruler, it was properly the hand of God that lifted him, for all human gifts and favour are God's. The verse displays the exaltation of the rejected one — Joseph, sold by his brethren, raised by God to save them — a type of Christ, rejected by his own and exalted by God to be their Savior. Stephen's induction continues: the very one the patriarchs rejected became, by God's providence, the deliverer of the nation. The God who was with Joseph in his afflictions raised him to glory and made the rejected brother the savior of those who sold him — the pattern fulfilled in Christ, whom God raised and exalted after his own had rejected him.
11:
"Now there came a dearth over all the land of Egypt and Chanaan, and great affliction: and our fathers found no sustenance." The brief verse notes the famine that drove Israel to Egypt. Barnes: "A famine (Genesis 41:54)... Jacob was living at that time in Canaan... No food; no means of living." The grammatical voices note the word for sustenance. Robertson: "Chortasmata... originally to feed with grass or herbs... includes food for both men and animals."

The Reformed Calvin draws out the providence in Joseph's prior exaltation: "Hereby it appeareth that the deliverance of Joseph was such a benefit as was common to all the family of Jacob. For, seeing the famine drew on, Joseph was sent before in due time to provide sustenance to feed the hungry... the free goodness of God appeareth plainly in the person of Joseph, whilst that he is appointed to nourish and feed his brethren, who had sold him."

Calvin also notes Stephen's theme: "Stephen putteth the Jews in mind of this, that the patriarchs were enforced to depart out of that land which was given them for an heritage, and that they died in another place... they were sojourners in it." The famine drove the patriarchs out of the promised land — pressing the point that they held it only as strangers.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: a famine came over Egypt and Canaan, and the patriarchs found no sustenance — the occasion that would drive Israel down to Egypt and to Joseph. Calvin draws out the providence: Joseph's prior exaltation (v.10) was the means by which God provided for the very brethren who had sold him, displaying God's free goodness in making the rejected one the preserver of his family. The verse also serves Stephen's theme: the patriarchs were driven out of the land given them for an inheritance, sojourners holding it only as strangers (Calvin) — pressing against the Jews' presumption on their possession of the land. The verse marks the famine that sets in motion the descent into Egypt, the bondage, and the eventual exodus — the great drama of redemption. The God who had exalted Joseph now uses the famine to bring the family to him, that the rejected brother might save them. The providence that seemed adverse (famine) was the means of preservation, through the very one the brethren had cast away.
12:
"But when Jacob heard that there was corn in Egypt, he sent out our fathers first." The brief verse notes Jacob sending his sons to Egypt. Barnes: "His ten sons; all his sons except Joseph and Benjamin (Genesis 42:2). Stephen here refers only to the history, without entering into details. By this general reference he sufficiently showed that he believed what Moses had spoken." The grammatical voices note "first" (the first of two journeys). Robertson: "While Jacob himself remained in Canaan before he went down to Egypt and died there."

The verse the witnesses read as part of Stephen's swift survey. He recounts the history without dwelling on details, showing his reverence for the Mosaic record (against the charge of blasphemy) and pressing his theme. The "corn in Egypt" — the grain Joseph had stored — was the providential provision that drew the family down.

The sending of the brothers the witnesses note as the beginning of the reunion and the fulfillment of God's purpose. The famine drove them to the very brother they had sold, who now (unknown to them) held the stores of Egypt — the providence working toward their preservation and his self-disclosure (v.13).

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Jacob, hearing of grain in Egypt, sent his sons (all but Benjamin) on the first of two journeys (Barnes, Robertson). Stephen recounts the history swiftly, without details, showing his reverence for the Mosaic record (against the blasphemy charge) and advancing his survey. The verse marks the beginning of the providential reunion: the famine driving the brothers to the very Joseph they had sold, who held the stores of Egypt unknown to them. The "corn in Egypt" — Joseph's stored grain — was God's provision drawing the family down, toward both their preservation and Joseph's self-disclosure (v.13). The verse displays the quiet working of providence: the brothers, who had cast Joseph away, are driven by famine to seek bread from him, fulfilling unknowingly the dreams they had despised (v.9). The God who had exalted Joseph now brings his brethren to him, that the rejected one might preserve them — the pattern that Stephen presses toward its application: the rejected Messiah is the one to whom Israel must come for life.
13:
"And at the second time Joseph was made known to his brethren; and Joseph's kindred was made known unto Pharaoh." The brief verse notes Joseph's self-disclosure. Gill: "when the brethren of Joseph went a second time down to Egypt for corn, Joseph made himself known unto them." Robertson: "This second visit is recorded in Genesis 45... In Genesis 41:12 the fact that Joseph was a Hebrew had been incidentally mentioned to Pharaoh, but now it was made clear to him."

The self-disclosure the witnesses read as the climax of the reconciliation. Joseph, the rejected brother now exalted, reveals himself to those who had sold him — not in vengeance but in mercy, to preserve them (Genesis 45). The making known of his kindred to Pharaoh prepared for the settling of the family in Egypt.

Chrysostom (under v.6) had drawn out Joseph's grace: "not to this point only did he carry his friendliness; he also made them known to Pharaoh, and brought them down into the land." The rejected one not only forgives but honours and provides for those who wronged him.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse of this section. The witnesses agree: at the second visit, Joseph made himself known to his brethren, and his kindred was made known to Pharaoh — the climax of the reconciliation (Gill, Robertson). The rejected brother, now exalted, reveals himself not in vengeance but in mercy, to preserve those who had sold him (Genesis 45), and honours them before Pharaoh, settling them in the land (Chrysostom). The verse displays the grace of the exalted Joseph toward his guilty brethren — forgiving, providing, exalting them — a type of Christ, who, rejected and exalted, reveals himself to his own for their salvation, not their condemnation. Stephen's induction presses toward its point: as Joseph made himself known to the brethren who rejected him, so the rejected Messiah is made known to Israel for their preservation. The self-disclosure of the rejected one, in mercy rather than vengeance, is the gospel pattern: Christ, whom his own rejected, reveals himself to save them. Joseph's grace foreshadows the greater grace of the risen Christ toward those who crucified him.
14:
"Then sent Joseph, and called his father Jacob to him, and all his kindred, threescore and fifteen souls." The witnesses note the descent of Jacob's family and a numerical crux. The difficulty: Genesis 46:27, Exodus 1:5, and Deuteronomy 10:22 give the number as seventy, but Stephen says seventy-five. Barnes: "Stephen has followed the account given by the Septuagint. In Genesis 46:27, that version reads... seventy-five souls. This number is made out by adding... nine souls [the sons of Joseph] to the sixty-six." Ellicott: "Stephen had the authority of the LXX. of Genesis 46:27, which gives the number at seventy-five, and makes it up by inserting the son and grandson of Manasseh, two sons and a grandson of Ephraim... Stephen, as a Hellenistic Jew, naturally accepted... the number which he found in the Greek version."

The grammatical voices note the textual basis. Robertson: "Stephen follows the LXX which counts some grandchildren of Joseph and so makes it 75 whereas Genesis 46:26 has 66 and then the next verse makes it 70." The difference is between the Hebrew and Greek texts, not an error of Stephen.

The Reformed Calvin discusses the textual history at length, concluding it is a copyist's variation: "I think that this difference came through the error of the writers which wrote out the books." He draws the lesson: "let us remember that it is not without cause that Paul doth forbid us to be too curious about genealogies... This, so small a number, is purposely expressed, to the end the power of God may the more plainly appear, in so great an enlarging of that kindred."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Joseph called Jacob and all his kindred — "threescore and fifteen souls" (75) — to Egypt. The number presents a crux: the Hebrew (Genesis 46:27; Exodus 1:5) gives seventy, but Stephen follows the LXX, which adds descendants of Joseph to make seventy-five (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson). This is a difference between the Hebrew and Greek texts, not an error of Stephen, who as a Hellenistic Jew naturally used the Greek version. Calvin treats it as a copyist's variation and draws the lesson against being over-curious about such numbers. The verse displays both a textual crux (testing inspiration theories) and Stephen's theme: the small number that descended into Egypt would, by God's power, become a great nation — the enlarging of the kindred a proof of divine power (Calvin). The verse marks the descent into Egypt, the seed of the nation; and the difference of number, the witnesses note, touches not the truth of the history but the choice of textual tradition. The God who multiplied seventy-five souls into a mighty people is the God of the promise to Abraham.
15:
"So Jacob went down into Egypt, and died, he, and our fathers," The brief verse notes the death of Jacob and the patriarchs in Egypt. Barnes: "The time which the Israelites remained in Egypt was two hundred and fifteen years; so that all the sons of Jacob were deceased before the Jews went out to go to the land of Canaan." Jacob and all his sons died in Egypt — in a strange land, not the land of promise.

The verse the witnesses (with Calvin, under v.11) read as part of Stephen's theme: the patriarchs died as sojourners in a foreign land. They held the promise but not the possession; they died strangers, awaiting the fulfillment in their seed. This presses against the Jews' presumption on the land.

The death of Jacob and the patriarchs in Egypt the witnesses note as setting the stage for the burial crux (v.16) and the long bondage that would follow. The family that went down seventy-five strong would die in Egypt and become, through the centuries of bondage, a great nation — the seed awaiting the exodus.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Jacob went down into Egypt and died there, as did all the patriarchs — in a strange land, before the possession of the promised land (Barnes). The verse advances Stephen's theme: the fathers died as sojourners, holding the promise but not the possession, strangers awaiting the fulfillment in their seed (Calvin) — pressing against the Jews' presumption on the land. The death of Jacob and the patriarchs in Egypt sets the stage for the burial in Canaan (v.16) and the long bondage to come. The verse marks the passing of the patriarchal generation: the family that descended into Egypt would die there, and from their seed, through centuries of affliction, God would raise the nation he had promised. The patriarchs died in faith, not having received the promises but having seen them afar off (Hebrews 11:13) — strangers and pilgrims on the earth, who held the land only by promise and died in a foreign land. Their burial in Canaan (v.16) would be the pledge of the inheritance their seed would possess. The God of the living was their God, though they died.
16:
"And were carried over into Sychem, and laid in the sepulchre that Abraham bought for a sum of money of the sons of Emmor the father of Sychem." The witnesses note one of the most discussed difficulties in the speech — the burial at Sychem in the field Abraham bought. The difficulty: Abraham bought Machpelah (Hebron) from the Hittites (Genesis 23); Jacob bought the field at Shechem from the sons of Hamor (Genesis 33:19; Joshua 24:32). The verse seems to conflate or confuse these.

The witnesses offer several solutions. (1) A textual error — "Abraham" for "Jacob." Robertson: "If Jacob is substituted for Abraham, the matter is cleared up... It is quite as likely that the word producing the error escaped from some early copyist as that so glaring an error was committed by Stephen" (Hackett). Calvin: "it is manifest that there is a fault in the word Abraham... this place must be amended." (2) Compression of two purchases. Wesley: "St. Stephen... contracts into one, two different sepulchres, places, and purchases." (3) A Samaritan tradition. Ellicott argues at length that Stephen, connected with Samaria, followed a Samaritan tradition carrying Shechem's associations back to Abraham, who built an altar there (Genesis 12:6-7) and may have bought the ground.

On the burial at Sychem itself, the witnesses note the Jewish vs Samaritan traditions. Barnes: Joseph's bones were buried at Shechem (Joshua 24:32); Josephus says the patriarchs were buried at Hebron, but "the Scriptures do not anywhere deny that the fathers were buried in Sychem." Ellicott notes the tombs of the twelve patriarchs were shown at Shechem in Jerome's time. Stephen's assertion of Shechem (the hated Samaritan site) may have inflamed his hearers.

The witnesses agree that this verse presents the chief crux of the speech — the burial at Sychem in the field "Abraham bought of the sons of Emmor." They divide on the solution: a textual error (Abraham for Jacob — Pearce, Hackett, Robertson, Calvin, who hold the text must be amended); a compression of two distinct purchases (Wesley); or Stephen's following of a Samaritan tradition carrying Shechem's associations back to Abraham (Ellicott). On the burial place, the witnesses note that while Josephus places the patriarchs at Hebron, Scripture nowhere denies Sychem, and a Samaritan tradition (the tombs shown there) supports it. The verse displays a real difficulty that tests theories of inspiration: whether a slip of memory, a copyist's error, or a Samaritan tradition, it does not impugn Luke's faithful report of the speech (Ellicott). Stephen's assertion of the hated Samaritan Shechem, claiming a brotherhood for Samaria, may itself have stoked the fury that ended in his martyrdom. The crux, however resolved, leaves the substance of the speech untouched: the fathers were buried in the land held only by promise.
17:
"But when the time of the promise drew nigh, which God had sworn to Abraham, the people grew and multiplied in Egypt," The witnesses note the multiplication of Israel as the time of deliverance approached. The puritan Henry (whose comment runs through the Moses section) marks Stephen's reverence: "let any one judge whether these are the words of one that was a blasphemer of Moses or no." The grammatical voices note the connection of promise and multiplication.

The Reformed Calvin (folded in) and the witnesses note the providence: as the time God had sworn to Abraham drew near, the people multiplied — the fulfillment of the promise beginning even amid the bondage. The growth of Israel in Egypt, despite the oppression, was the working of God toward the promised deliverance.

On "the time of the promise," the witnesses note the divine appointment. The deliverance came not by chance but at the time God had sworn — the four hundred years (v.6) drawing to their appointed end. The multiplication of the people prepared the nation that God would lead out.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which opens the Moses section. The witnesses agree: as the time of the promise sworn to Abraham drew near, Israel multiplied in Egypt — the fulfillment of God's word beginning even amid the bondage. The growth of the people, despite oppression, was the providence of God working toward the promised deliverance at its appointed time (Calvin). Henry marks Stephen's evident reverence for Moses throughout this section, answering the charge of blasphemy. The verse displays the faithfulness of God to his promise: at the appointed season, the seed of Abraham became a great people, ready to be led out. The multiplication that the oppressing Pharaoh feared (v.18) was the very fulfillment of God's covenant — the more they were afflicted, the more they multiplied (Exodus 1:12). The verse marks the turn from the patriarchs to Moses, from the descent into Egypt to the eve of the exodus: God's promise, sworn to Abraham, now drawing toward its fulfillment in the deliverance of a multiplied people. The God who keeps covenant was preparing, in the growth of Israel under bondage, the nation he would redeem.
18:
"Till another king arose, which knew not Joseph." The brief verse notes the rise of a new king hostile to Israel. The grammatical voices identify him. Robertson: "A different kind of king also, probably a king of the new dynasty after the shepherd kings had been expelled from Egypt... Joseph's history and services meant nothing to the new king." Barnes notes the common name Pharaoh was a title, and that "this expression probably means that he did not favour the designs of Joseph; he did not remember the benefits he had conferred."

On "knew not Joseph," the witnesses note the ingratitude. Clarke: "did not approve of him, of his mode of governing the kingdom, nor of his people, nor of his God." Barnes: "National ingratitude and forgetfulness of favours have not been uncommon in the world; and a change of dynasty or succession has often obliterated all memory of former obligations."

The new king the witnesses read as the turn from favour to oppression. The benefits Joseph had conferred were forgotten; the people he had saved became, under the new king, the object of fear and cruelty (vv.19). The rise of the hostile king marks the beginning of the bondage that would last until the exodus.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: a new king arose who "knew not Joseph" — probably of a new dynasty (after the expulsion of the shepherd kings, Robertson), to whom Joseph's services meant nothing, and who did not favour his people (Barnes, Clarke). The forgetting of Joseph marks national ingratitude and the turn from favour to oppression: the benefits Joseph had conferred were obliterated by the change of dynasty, and the people he had saved became the object of fear and cruelty (v.19). The verse marks the beginning of the bondage — the affliction foretold to Abraham (v.6) now coming upon his seed. The God who had exalted Joseph and multiplied his people now permitted the rise of the oppressor, in the working of his providence toward the exodus. The forgetting of Joseph by the new king is the human ingratitude through which God's larger purpose advanced: the bondage that would issue in the great deliverance. The verse turns the history toward the affliction of Israel and the raising up of Moses, the deliverer — the rejected one whom the people themselves would also reject (vv.25-27).
19:
"The same dealt subtilly with our kindred, and evil entreated our fathers, so that they cast out their young children, to the end they might not live." The witnesses note the cunning cruelty of Pharaoh against Israel. The grammatical voices stress the craft. Robertson: "Dealt subtilly (katasophisamenos)... to play the sophist... To use fraud, craft, deceit." Clarke notes the Egyptians' reputation: "the Egyptians were so remarkable [for craft] that to Egyptize signified to act cunningly."

On the casting out of children, Robertson: "Pharaoh required the Israelites to expose their children to death... But the Israelites fought against such an iniquity." The command to drown the male children (Exodus 1:22) was the height of the cunning cruelty — designed to weaken and destroy the nation.

The Reformed Calvin draws out the tyranny and its providential frustration: "Pharaoh did craftily invent new shifts and wicked pretenses, that he might lay heavier burdens upon the people, like as almost all tyrants do... such violent barbarism did the more set forth the unlooked-for and incredible power of God; because when Pharaoh hath, by all means possible, striven against God, yet all is in vain." The very cruelty that sought to destroy Israel set the stage for God's power in their deliverance.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the new king dealt cunningly and cruelly with Israel, oppressing them and commanding the casting out of their male children — the height of his sophistical cruelty (Robertson, Clarke), designed to weaken and destroy the nation. Calvin draws out the tyranny: Pharaoh, like all tyrants, invented pretexts for heavier burdens; yet his violent barbarism only set forth the more the incredible power of God, who frustrated all his striving. The verse displays the depth of the affliction foretold to Abraham (v.6) — the people near to destruction by the murderous edict — and the providence that overruled it. The cruelty that sought to drown the nation's future was the very occasion of God's raising up Moses (v.20), the deliverer preserved from the waters. The verse marks the extremity of the bondage, when the seed of Abraham seemed at the point of annihilation; yet at that very moment God prepared the deliverer. The malice of Pharaoh, striving against God, served only to magnify the deliverance to come — for the more Israel was afflicted, the more God's power would be displayed in their rescue.
20:
"In which time Moses was born, and was exceeding fair, and nourished up in his father's house three months:" The witnesses note the birth of Moses at the height of the persecution. On "exceeding fair," the grammatical voices explain the Hebraism. Barnes: "Greek, was fair to God; properly rendered, was very handsome. The word God is used in the Greek here in accordance with the Hebrew usage, by which anything that is very handsome, or lofty, or grand, is thus designated." Ellicott notes the LXX of Exodus 2:2 and the idiom; Josephus describes the infant Moses' beauty as such "that those who met him turned to gaze in admiration."

The grammatical voices connect it to Hebrews 11:23. Robertson: "Asteios... only twice in the N.T. (here and Hebrews 11:23) and both times about Moses." The beauty of the child moved his parents to hide him (Exodus 2:2; Hebrews 11:23).

The Reformed Calvin marks the providential timing: "Moses was born at the very same time when the king had commanded that all the men children should be cast out. Therefore, it seemeth that the minister of deliverance is dead before he is born. But that time is most fit for God to work in, when there is no hope or counsel to be looked for at man's hands... God doth make perfect his power in man's weakness."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Moses was born at the height of the persecution, "exceeding fair" — the Hebraism "fair to God" denoting surpassing beauty (Barnes, Ellicott), the same word used of him in Hebrews 11:23 — and hidden by his parents three months. Calvin marks the providential timing: the deliverer born at the very moment the murderous edict raged, when no human hope remained — for God works best where man's help fails, perfecting his power in weakness. The verse displays the providence that raises up the deliverer at the darkest hour: when Pharaoh sought to drown the nation's future, God gave Moses, and moved his parents by the child's beauty to preserve him by faith (Hebrews 11:23). The verse marks the birth of the deliverer amid the bondage — the minister of redemption preserved from the waters that were meant to destroy. The God who frustrates the tyrant (v.19) brings forth, at the moment of greatest danger, the one who would lead Israel out. The beautiful child, hidden and preserved, is the dawn of deliverance — born under the shadow of death, kept alive by God's providence and his parents' faith.
21:
"And when he was cast out, Pharaoh's daughter took him up, and nourished him for her own son." The witnesses note the exposure and adoption of Moses. Gill recounts the ark of bulrushes and the watching sister (Exodus 2:3). Robertson notes the meaning of "took up": "the word was used of old for picking up exposed children... Adopted is the idea here. After the birth of a child the father took it up to his bosom, if he meant to rear it; otherwise it was doomed to perish."

On "nourished him for her own son," the witnesses note the adoption and its consequences. Robertson: "she nursed him up for herself as a son... The tradition is that she designed Moses for the throne as the Pharaoh had no son." Wesley: "By which means, being designed for a kingdom, he had all those advantages of education, which he could not have had, if he had not been exposed."

The adoption the witnesses read as the providence that prepared Moses for his work. Cast out under the murderous edict, he was taken up by Pharaoh's own daughter and reared in the palace — the very court of the oppressor becoming the school of the deliverer. The exposure that should have meant death became the means of his elevation and education.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Moses, cast out under the edict, was taken up by Pharaoh's daughter and reared as her own son (Exodus 2:5,10) — "took up" the term for adopting an exposed child (Robertson). The adoption was the providence that prepared the deliverer: cast out to die, he was reared in the palace of the oppressor, perhaps designed for the throne (Robertson), gaining an education he could not otherwise have had (Wesley). The verse displays the overruling of God: the very court of Pharaoh, who sought to destroy Israel, became the school of Israel's deliverer. The exposure meant for death became the means of Moses' elevation; the daughter of the tyrant nursed the one who would overthrow the tyrant's house. Stephen's induction continues: God prepares his deliverer in unlikely ways, through the providence that turns the enemy's house into the cradle of redemption. The God who preserved Moses from the waters now establishes him in the palace, equipping him for the deliverance to come — the rejected child made a prince, that he might become the savior of his people.
22:
"And Moses was learned in all the wisdom of the Egyptians, and was mighty in words and in deeds." The witnesses note Moses' education and eminence. On the wisdom, Barnes: "the learning of the Egyptians was confined chiefly to astrology, to the interpretation of dreams, to medicine, to mathematics, and to their sacred science... not unfrequently spoken of in the Scriptures (1 Kings 4:30)." Robertson: "The priestly caste in Egypt was noted for their knowledge of science, astronomy, medicine, and mathematics... Moses, like Paul, was a man of the schools."

Ellicott draws out the broader significance: "The passage is instructive (1) as an indirect plea on the part of Stephen, like that afterwards made by Clement of Alexandria and Justin, for the recognition of heathen wisdom as an element in the divine education of mankind; (2) as having contributed to fix the attention of the more cultivated... early Christian critics... on the teaching of Greek poets and philosophers." On "mighty in words," Ellicott reconciles with Exodus 4:10 ("not eloquent"): "the utterance of a true humility shrinking from the burden of a mighty task."

The Reformed Calvin notes the gifts as God's preparation: "because God had determined to redeem his people, he doth, in the mean season, frame both the mind of Moses and all other things to finish his work." Yet he guards against the corruptions of Egyptian wisdom, noting Moses set forth in the Scriptures only what pertains to godliness, descending to the common capacity.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Moses was learned in all the wisdom of the Egyptians (their science, astronomy, medicine, mathematics, Barnes, Robertson) and mighty in words and deeds. Ellicott draws out the significance: Stephen's note is an indirect plea, like the later Fathers', for heathen wisdom as an element in the divine education of mankind. The "mighty in words" is reconciled with Moses' confessed slowness of speech (Exodus 4:10) as the utterance of humility before a great task (Ellicott). Calvin notes the gifts as God's preparation of his chosen deliverer, while guarding against the corruptions of Egyptian learning. The verse displays the equipping of Moses: the rejected child, reared in Pharaoh's court, made learned and eminent — prepared by God, through the providence of his adoption, for the work of deliverance. Like Paul, Moses was a man of the schools, his learning and power the instruments God would use. The verse marks the full preparation of the deliverer; yet, as the next verses show (vv.23-27), this prepared and powerful man would be rejected by the very people he came to save — the type of Christ, mighty in word and deed, rejected by his own.
23:
"And when he was full forty years old, it came into his heart to visit his brethren the children of Israel." The witnesses note Moses' turning to his brethren at forty. On the age, the grammatical voices note the Jewish tradition. Barnes: "It is a constant tradition of the Jews that Moses was forty years of age... Moses lived in the palace of Pharaoh forty years; he was forty years in Midian; and he ministered to Israel forty years." Robertson: "The life of Moses is divided into three periods of forty years each."

On "it came into his heart," the grammatical voices note the Hebrew image. Robertson: "Came up as if from the lower deeps of his nature. This Hebrew image occurs in Jeremiah 3:16... To visit (episkepsasthai)... to go to see for oneself, to help if possible." The visit was a purposeful going to see and aid his oppressed people.

The Reformed Calvin reads it as a new motion of the Spirit: "the Spirit of God did at length awake his mind, as it were out of sleep, that he might at length go visit his brethren, whom he had long time neglected... this new care for his brethren which came into his mind, proceeded from a new and unwonted motion of God's Spirit." The turning to his people, after forty years in the palace, was God's stirring of the deliverer.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: at forty (the first of the three forty-year periods of his life, Robertson, by Jewish tradition), it came into Moses' heart to visit his brethren — a purposeful going to see and aid his oppressed people (Robertson). Calvin reads it as a new motion of the Spirit, awakening Moses from his long neglect in the palace to care for his kindred. The verse marks the turning of the deliverer toward his people: after forty years in Pharaoh's court, Moses' heart was stirred (by the Spirit, Calvin) to identify with his afflicted brethren — the choice that Hebrews 11:24-26 celebrates, refusing to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter and choosing to suffer with the people of God. The verse begins the account of Moses' first attempt at deliverance (vv.24-25), which the people would reject (v.27) — the rejected deliverer prefiguring Christ. The God who had prepared Moses in the palace now stirs his heart to seek his brethren, beginning the long path to the exodus, through rejection, exile, and the burning bush.
24:
"And seeing one of them suffer wrong, he defended him, and avenged him that was oppressed, and smote the Egyptian:" The witnesses note Moses' first act on behalf of his people. Barnes: "The wrong... was, that the Egyptian was smiting the Hebrew (Exodus 2:11,12)... He slew him, and buried him in the sand." The grammatical voices note the words. Ellicott: "And avenged him. The Greek phrase is noticeable as identical with that used by St. Luke (Luke 18:7) in reporting the lesson drawn by our Lord from the parable of the Unjust Judge."

The Reformed Calvin reads the deed as Moses' divinely-appointed first act of deliverance: "Moses came not to this spectacle by chance, but forasmuch as God had appointed him to be the deliverer of his people, he would have him show forth this token, and, as it were, make this beginning... unless God had armed him, it had been a thing altogether unlawful for him to kill any man." He notes "this heroical courage and nobleness of heart was a work of the Holy Ghost; because God doth mightily show forth his power in those whom he appointeth unto great matters."

The smiting of the Egyptian the witnesses read as the first token of Moses' calling — the deliverer beginning his work, though prematurely and not yet recognized. Calvin: "Moses was even then offered to be the minister of deliverance when the day was at hand, according to the covenant made with Abraham, yet did the people hope for nothing less."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Moses, seeing a Hebrew wronged by an Egyptian, defended him and slew the Egyptian (Exodus 2:11-12) — his first act on behalf of his people. Calvin reads it as the divinely-appointed first token of Moses' calling as deliverer: not a private murder, but the heroic act of one God had armed for great matters, the work of the Holy Spirit. The deed offered Moses as the minister of deliverance, though the people hoped for nothing of the kind (Calvin). The verse displays the beginning of Moses' deliverance-work — premature, unrecognized, and soon to be rejected (v.27). The deliverer, stirred to care for his brethren (v.23), takes his first action; but the people would not understand (v.25), and the deliverer would be driven into exile (v.29). Stephen's induction presses on: the God-appointed deliverer, beginning his work, is rejected by the very people he came to save — the pattern fulfilled in Christ. The smiting of the Egyptian, the first token of Moses' calling, leads not to recognition but to rejection and forty years of exile, before the burning bush would send him back.
25:
"For he supposed his brethren would have understood how that God by his hand would deliver them: but they understood not." The witnesses note Moses' expectation and the people's failure to understand. Barnes: "When they saw him alone contending with the Egyptian, when it was understood that he had come and taken vengeance on one of their oppressors, it might have been presumed that he regarded himself as directed by God to interpose, and save the people." Lightfoot adds the Jewish tradition that Moses had a prophetic spirit and knew himself designed to redeem Israel.

The grammatical voices note the rhetorical force. Robertson: "Page notes the rhetorical power of these words from Stephen." The contrast between Moses' supposing they would understand and their not understanding is the heart of the verse — the deliverer offered, the people blind.

The failure to understand the witnesses read as the people's rejection of their God-appointed deliverer. Moses, knowing himself called, supposed his brethren would recognize that God was delivering them by his hand; but they understood not — the first rejection of Moses, prefiguring the rejection of the greater Deliverer. The blindness of the people delayed their deliverance forty years (v.30).

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Moses supposed his brethren would understand that God was delivering them by his hand, but they understood not — the rhetorical heart of Stephen's point (Robertson). Moses, knowing himself called (perhaps by a prophetic spirit, Lightfoot), expected recognition; the people's blindness was their failure to receive their God-appointed deliverer (Barnes). The verse displays the rejection of the deliverer by his own — the first of Moses' rejections, which Stephen presses as the pattern of Israel's history (and the indictment of his hearers): as the fathers rejected Moses, so they now reject the Messiah. The blindness of the people, who did not understand that God was sending them salvation, delayed the deliverance forty years and drove Moses into exile (v.29). Stephen's induction reaches its sharpest point in the Moses narrative: the deliverer, mighty and God-sent, rejected by the very people he came to save — exactly as Israel had now rejected Jesus, whom God sent to deliver them. The people who understood not their deliverer are the type of those who knew not the day of their visitation in Christ.
26:
"And the next day he shewed himself unto them as they strove, and would have set them at one again, saying, Sirs, ye are brethren; why do ye wrong one to another?" The witnesses note Moses' attempt to reconcile two striving Hebrews. Barnes: "As they were engaged in a quarrel... Greek, would have urged them to peace. This he did by remonstrating with the man that did the wrong." The grammatical voices note the conative imperfect. Robertson: "he tried to reconcile them (or change them into peace)... It is the conative imperfect active."

On "ye are brethren," Barnes draws the lesson: "You belong not only to the same nation, but you are brethren and companions in affliction, and should not therefore contend with each other... it is from this class that contentions and lawsuits usually arise. The address... might be applied to the whole human family."

The Reformed Calvin draws out the people's fault in rejecting Moses' good offices: "Stephen declareth now that the fathers did not only neglect, but maliciously reject the grace of God... the people themselves were in the fault, that they were no sooner delivered and eased... refusing the grace of God in the person of one man, they cause the time of their deliverance to be deferred forty years." And the lesson for God's servants: "the servants of God cannot so do their duty in reproving the vices of men, but they shall suffer many injuries... when they do well, they shall surely hear evil."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the next day Moses found two Hebrews striving and tried to reconcile them — "ye are brethren, why do ye wrong one to another?" (a conative attempt at peace, Robertson). The appeal to brotherhood (Barnes) is the deliverer's good office, seeking the unity of his afflicted people. Calvin draws out the people's malicious rejection of this grace: by refusing Moses' good offices, they delayed their own deliverance forty years; and he notes the lesson that God's servants, even in doing good, will suffer injury and hear evil. The verse displays the deliverer's loving attempt to reconcile his people, and the beginning of their rejection of him (v.27). Stephen presses the pattern: Moses, seeking the peace and good of his brethren, is rebuffed — as the prophets, and now the Messiah, were rejected by those they came to bless. The verse marks the second rejection of Moses (after v.25), leading to his exile (v.29). The deliverer who sought to make his brethren one is thrust away by the very people he came to help — the type of Christ, who came to his own, and his own received him not.
27:
"But he that did his neighbour wrong thrust him away, saying, Who made thee a ruler and a judge over us?" The witnesses note the rejection of Moses by the wrongdoer. Barnes: "Intent on his purpose, filled with rage and passion, he rejected all interference... It is usually the man that does the injury that is unwilling to be reconciled." Robertson: "It is always the man who is doing the wrong who is hard to reconcile."

On "who made thee a ruler and a judge," Ellicott draws out the significance: "The stress laid on this afterwards, in Acts 7:35, shows that it took its place in the induction which was to show that the whole history of Israel had been marked by the rejection of those who were, at each successive stage, God's ministers... and that the rejection of Jesus was therefore a presumptive proof that He, too, was sent from God." The rejection of Moses is the pattern of the rejection of Christ.

Wesley notes the recurring excuse: "Under the presence of the want of a call by man, the instruments of God are often rejected." The wrongdoer rejects Moses on the pretext that he had no authority — the common cover of those who resist God's messengers.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the wrongdoer thrust Moses away — "Who made thee a ruler and a judge over us?" — the rejection of the God-appointed deliverer (Barnes, Robertson, the wrongdoer always hardest to reconcile). Ellicott draws out the key: Stephen lays stress on this rejection (repeated at v.35) as the pattern of Israel's whole history — the rejection of God's ministers at each stage, and so a presumptive proof that the rejected Jesus, too, was sent from God. Wesley notes the pretext: God's instruments are often rejected on the cover of lacking a human call. The verse displays the heart of Stephen's induction: the deliverer rejected by his own, on the very pretext his hearers used against Christ. As the fathers said to Moses "Who made thee a ruler?" so the council rejected Jesus' authority. The verse marks the decisive rejection of Moses that drove him into exile (v.29) — and stands as the type of the rejection of the Messiah. The God-sent deliverer, repudiated by the people he came to save, is the recurring pattern that convicts Stephen's hearers: they have done to Christ what their fathers did to Moses.
28:
"Wilt thou kill me, as thou diddest the Egyptian yesterday?" The brief verse notes the wrongdoer's threat, revealing that Moses' deed was known. Gill: "dost thou design to kill me?" Barnes: "How it was known that he had killed the Egyptian does not appear. It was probably communicated by the man who was rescued from the hands of the Egyptian (Exodus 2:11,12)." Robertson notes the form: "Expecting the answer no, but a thrust direct at Moses... Do you wish to kill me?"

The threat the witnesses read as the exposure of Moses' deed and the deepening of his rejection. The wrongdoer not only repudiates Moses' authority (v.27) but throws his slaying of the Egyptian in his face — revealing that the act, which Moses supposed secret (Exodus 2:12), was known. This exposure would drive Moses to flee (v.29).

The verse the witnesses note as the turning point: Moses' first attempt at deliverance, met with rejection and the threat of betrayal, ends in his flight. The deliverer, repudiated and exposed, must leave his people for forty years before God would send him back (v.30).

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse of this section. The witnesses agree: the wrongdoer threw Moses' slaying of the Egyptian in his face — "Wilt thou kill me, as thou diddest the Egyptian yesterday?" — revealing that the deed, supposed secret (Exodus 2:12), was known (Barnes), and deepening the rejection of the deliverer. The threat exposed Moses and would drive him to flee (v.29). The verse marks the turning point: Moses' first attempt at deliverance, met with repudiation (v.27) and now the threat of betrayal, ends in failure and exile. The deliverer, rejected and exposed by his own people, must leave them for forty years before the burning bush would send him back (v.30). Stephen presses the pattern: the God-sent deliverer, instead of being received, is threatened and driven away by those he came to save — as the prophets were persecuted, and the Messiah crucified. The verse displays the depth of the people's rejection: not only refusing Moses' authority but turning his own act of deliverance against him. The rejected deliverer flees; but God's purpose, deferred, would be fulfilled in his own time, through the very one the people had cast away.
29:
"Then fled Moses at this saying, and was a stranger in the land of Madian, where he begat two sons." The witnesses note Moses' flight to Midian. Gill: "he knew his life was in the utmost danger." Barnes: "Moses fled because he now ascertained that it was known... as soon as Pharaoh heard of it, he sought to take his life (Exodus 2:15)." On Midian, Barnes: "a part of Arabia... on the east side of the Red Sea... This was extensively a desert region, an unknown land; and Moses expected there to be safe."

The grammatical voices note "stranger" (sojourner). Robertson: "Temporary dweller (cf. Abraham in verse 6) in Midian though for forty years." Moses, the rejected deliverer, became a sojourner in a foreign land — like Abraham, a stranger.

On the two sons, Barnes: "He married Zipporah, the daughter of Reuel, or Jethro, a priest of Midian. The names of the two sons were Gershom and Eliezer (Exodus 18:3,4)." The forty years in Midian (the second period of Moses' life) were his exile and preparation.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Moses, his deed exposed and his life threatened, fled to Midian, where he became a sojourner and begat two sons (Exodus 2:15; 18:3-4). The flight was the consequence of his rejection: the deliverer, repudiated by his people and hunted by Pharaoh, became a stranger in a foreign land (like Abraham, Robertson) for forty years — his exile and preparation, the second of the three forty-year periods of his life. The verse displays the deliverer in exile: rejected by his own, driven from Egypt, dwelling as a stranger in Midian, awaiting God's time. Stephen's induction notes the long delay: the deliverance, deferred by the people's rejection of Moses, would wait forty years until the burning bush (v.30). The verse marks the low point of Moses' story — the rejected deliverer a fugitive and sojourner — yet also the providence that prepared him: the forty years in the wilderness shaping the shepherd who would lead Israel through the wilderness. The God who let Moses be rejected and exiled was preparing him still, that in his own time he might send him back to deliver the people who had thrust him away.
30:
"And when forty years were expired, there appeared to him in the wilderness of mount Sina an angel of the Lord in a flame of fire in a bush." The witnesses note the burning bush after Moses' forty years in Midian. Gill: "so long the Jews say Moses kept Jethro's flock, and so many years he lived in Midian... he was now fourscore years of age." The puritan Henry (whose comment opens here) marks Stephen's reverence: "let any one judge whether these are the words of one that was a blasphemer of Moses or no; nothing could be spoken more honourably."

The grammatical voices note the appearance. The "angel of the Lord in a flame of fire in a bush" was the manifestation by which God called Moses (Exodus 3:2) — the second great theophany of the speech (after the appearance to Abraham, v.2). The witnesses note that this appearance, like that to Abraham, was in a strange land, not the temple — pressing Stephen's theme that God's presence is not bound to place.

The forty years the witnesses note as the completion of Moses' preparation. After forty years in the palace and forty in exile, Moses, now eighty, is called at the burning bush to the work he had attempted prematurely (v.24). The God who let him be rejected and exiled now sends him back as the deliverer — in God's time, not man's.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which opens the burning-bush section. The witnesses agree: after forty years in Midian (Moses now eighty, Gill), the angel of the Lord appeared to him in a flame of fire in a bush at Sinai (Exodus 3:2) — the second great theophany of the speech, by which God called Moses to deliverance. Henry marks Stephen's evident reverence for Moses, answering the charge of blasphemy. The appearance, like that to Abraham (v.2), was in a strange land, not the temple — pressing Stephen's theme that God reveals himself where he will, not bound to place. The verse marks the completion of Moses' preparation and his calling: the rejected and exiled deliverer, after forty years, is sent back by God in his own time. The God who let Moses attempt deliverance prematurely (v.24), be rejected (v.27), and flee into exile (v.29), now calls him at the burning bush to the work for which he was prepared. The verse displays the patience of God's timing and the manifestation of his presence in the wilderness — and continues Stephen's great survey toward its climax: the deliverer rejected, exiled, and at last sent by God to redeem the people who had thrust him away. The burning bush is the turning point: the long-deferred deliverance is at hand.
31:
On Acts 7:31 — “When Moses saw it, he wondered at the sight: and as he drew near to behold it, the voice of the Lord came unto him” — the witnesses mark the wonder of the burning bush.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the sign and the voice. Gill: to see “a bush on fire, and yet not consumed… was wonderful indeed.” Robertson observes that “the angel of Jehovah of verse 30 is termed Jehovah himself,” and that “Jesus makes powerful use of these words” (Matthew 22:32). Ellicott marks that the speech, with Exodus 3:4, ascribes the voice to the Lord, the visible manifestation to the angel of the Lord. The Reformed Calvin: God so dealt with the fathers “that they might assuredly know his majesty,” distinguishing his visions from “the juggling casts of Satan.”

The verse, within Stephen’s long rehearsal of Israel’s history, recalls the bush that burned unconsumed. Moses, drawn by the marvel, hears the voice of the Lord — the God of his fathers speaking from the flame.

So the witnesses see Stephen recount the call of Moses as part of his great argument before the Sanhedrin. The burning bush, the witnesses note, was the token of God’s self-revelation to Moses — a sign that arrested him and a voice that commissioned him. They mark Robertson’s observation that the “angel of the Lord” who spoke is here named Jehovah, the same identification on which Christ would later build his proof of the resurrection. Calvin draws the point that God’s visions carry their authority by the word joined to them, distinguishing true revelation from satanic counterfeit. The verse thus advances Stephen’s sweeping survey: God revealed himself and his purpose to Moses at the bush, the deliverer who would lead Israel out — and, as the speech will press, the type of a greater Deliverer whom the nation has now rejected, even as their fathers once refused Moses.

So the witnesses see Stephen recount the call of Moses as part of his great argument before the Sanhedrin. The burning bush was the token of God’s self-revelation — a sign that arrested Moses and a voice that commissioned him. They mark Robertson’s observation that the “angel of the Lord” is here named Jehovah, the same identification on which Christ built his proof of the resurrection. The verse advances Stephen’s survey: God revealed himself to Moses the deliverer — the type of a greater Deliverer whom the nation has now rejected.
32:
On Acts 7:32 — “Saying, I am the God of thy fathers, the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob. Then Moses trembled, and durst not behold” — the witnesses mark the self-naming of God.

The grammatical-historical commentators connect it with Christ’s own use. Barnes points to Matthew 22:32, where this verse proves the resurrection; Ellicott notes that Stephen, perhaps one of the Seventy, may have known the Lord had cited these words “against the unbelief of the Sadducees.” The Reformed Calvin marks the purpose: “bare visions should do but little good, unless doctrine were joined”; and God, calling himself the God of the fathers, recalls his covenant.

The verse gives the words from the bush. God names himself the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; and Moses, awed, dares not look. The covenant-keeping God of the patriarchs reveals himself to the deliverer he is sending.

So the witnesses dwell on the covenant force of God’s self-naming. By calling himself the God of the fathers, God declares his faithfulness to the promises made to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and grounds the deliverance of Israel in that ancient covenant. The witnesses note the deeper truth Christ drew from these very words: that God is the God of the living, so that the patriarchs live to him and shall be raised — a point not lost, Ellicott suggests, on Stephen, who knew the Lord’s use of the text against the Sadducees. They mark Moses’ trembling reverence before the divine majesty. The verse thus binds the call of Moses to the covenant with the fathers, showing that the God who delivered Israel is the faithful, living God of the promises — the same God whose greater salvation, Stephen will argue, the nation now resists in rejecting the Christ.

So the witnesses dwell on the covenant force of God’s self-naming. By calling himself the God of the fathers, God declares his faithfulness to the promises and grounds Israel’s deliverance in the ancient covenant. The witnesses note the deeper truth Christ drew from these words: that God is the God of the living, so the patriarchs live to him and shall be raised — a point not lost on Stephen. They mark Moses’ trembling reverence. The God who delivered Israel is the faithful, living God whose greater salvation the nation now resists in rejecting Christ.
33:
On Acts 7:33 — “Then said the Lord to him, Put off thy shoes from thy feet: for the place where thou standest is holy ground” — the witnesses mark the call to reverence.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the act and its meaning. Barnes: to put off the shoes “was an act of reverence,” the ancients not entering a holy place shod. Robertson: the priests ministered barefoot, and Moslems enter mosques so today (Joshua 5:15); “even the ground near the bush was holy, a fine example for Stephen’s argument.” The Reformed Calvin: the Lord meant “to lift up the mind of Moses into heaven, that he might not think upon any earthly thing.” Wesley: “the holiness of places depends on the peculiar presence of God there.”

The verse gives the command of reverence at the bush. Moses must remove his sandals, for the ground is holy — made holy not in itself but by the presence of God. The deliverer is taught awe before the God who commissions him.

So the witnesses see in the unshod feet a lesson in reverence that serves Stephen’s larger point.
