The witnesses note the telling detail Robertson presses: that a patch of desert ground, far from any temple, was “holy ground” because God was there. This was a fine stroke in Stephen’s argument against those who confined God’s presence to the temple: God had hallowed a bush in the wilderness long before Solomon built his house. They mark Wesley’s principle that the holiness of places depends on God’s presence, not the place itself; and Calvin’s, that the command was to lift Moses’ mind from earth to heaven. The verse thus quietly undercuts the charge that Stephen had blasphemed the holy place: holiness belongs to God wherever he reveals himself, and the temple has no monopoly on his presence — a truth that prepares the climax of the speech about the Most High who dwells not in temples made with hands.

So the witnesses see in the unshod feet a lesson in reverence that serves Stephen’s larger point. The witnesses note the telling detail Robertson presses: a patch of desert ground, far from any temple, was “holy” because God was there. This was a fine stroke against those who confined God’s presence to the temple: God hallowed a bush in the wilderness long before Solomon built his house. Holiness belongs to God wherever he reveals himself, and the temple has no monopoly on his presence — preparing the climax about the Most High who dwells not in temples made with hands.
34:
On Acts 7:34 — “I have seen, I have seen the affliction of my people… and am come down to deliver them. And now come, I will send thee into Egypt” — the witnesses mark the commissioning of Moses.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the emphatic Hebrew. Robertson: “I have surely seen” (idon eidon) imitates the Hebrew infinitive absolute (Exodus 3:7). Barnes: the repetition is “emphatic”; “am come down” means God “was about to deliver them.” The Reformed Calvin: God “is said to see our miseries when he hath respect to us, and is careful for our safety.” Gill marks the “exquisite and exact knowledge the Lord took of the affliction of his people.”

The verse gives God’s compassion and commission. He has surely seen his people’s affliction, heard their groaning, and come down to deliver them — and he sends Moses as his instrument. The God of the fathers acts to redeem his oppressed people.

So the witnesses see in this verse the heart of God toward his suffering people and the sending of their deliverer. The doubled “I have seen,” the witnesses note, is the Hebrew way of stressing the certainty and depth of God’s regard; he is no distant deity but one who beholds, hears, and comes down to save. They mark that God’s “coming down” is his stooping to deliver, and that he chooses to work through a sent man, Moses. The verse thus advances Stephen’s parallel: as God sent Moses to redeem Israel from Egypt, so he has sent a greater Deliverer, the Christ, to redeem from a worse bondage. The compassion that saw Israel’s affliction is the same that sent the Saviour; and the nation that now rejects the Christ, Stephen implies, is repeating its fathers’ refusal of the deliverer God in mercy sent.

So the witnesses see in this verse the heart of God toward his suffering people and the sending of their deliverer. The doubled “I have seen” stresses the certainty and depth of God’s regard; he is no distant deity but one who beholds, hears, and comes down to save, choosing to work through a sent man. The verse advances Stephen’s parallel: as God sent Moses to redeem Israel from Egypt, so he has sent a greater Deliverer to redeem from a worse bondage. The nation that now rejects Christ is repeating its fathers’ refusal of the deliverer God in mercy sent.
35:
On Acts 7:35 — “This Moses whom they refused… the same did God send to be a ruler and a deliverer by the hand of the angel which appeared to him in the bush” — the witnesses mark the great parallel of Moses and Christ.

The Eastern fathers press the type. Chrysostom: “This Moses… the man whom they had set at naught… this same, God… sent unto them… just as they themselves now.” The grammatical-historical commentators note the rhetoric. Robertson: “This Moses” — a sixfold repetition (anaphora), for “clearly Stephen means to draw a parallel between Moses and Jesus.” Ellicott: “ruler and redeemer,” the word chosen “to emphasise the parallelism” with Christ. Wesley: a deliverer is “much more than a judge.”

The verse turns the history into argument. The very Moses whom Israel rejected — “who made thee a ruler?” — God made their ruler and redeemer. Rejection by the people did not annul, but preceded, God’s appointment. The pattern is unmistakable.

So the witnesses see Stephen drive home the parallel that is the burden of his whole speech. The emphatic “This Moses,” repeated again and again, fastens attention on the rejected-yet-exalted deliverer; for, as the fathers and expositors agree, Stephen is drawing the figure of Christ. Israel refused Moses, saying “who made thee a ruler?” — yet God sent that same Moses as ruler and redeemer; just so the nation has refused Jesus, whom God has made Lord and Christ. The witnesses note Ellicott’s point that the very word “redeemer” was chosen to mark the parallel. The verse thus exposes the recurring pattern of Israel’s history: the deliverer God sends is first rejected by the people. Stephen turns the Sanhedrin’s reverence for Moses against them, showing that in rejecting the Christ they do as their fathers did to Moses — and so resist the God who sent them both.

So the witnesses see Stephen drive home the parallel that is the burden of his whole speech. The emphatic “This Moses,” repeated again and again, fastens attention on the rejected-yet-exalted deliverer; for Stephen is drawing the figure of Christ. Israel refused Moses, saying “who made thee a ruler?” — yet God sent that same Moses as ruler and redeemer; just so the nation has refused Jesus, whom God has made Lord and Christ. Stephen turns the Sanhedrin’s reverence for Moses against them: in rejecting the Christ they do as their fathers did to Moses.
36:
On Acts 7:36 — “He brought them out, after that he had shewed wonders and signs in the land of Egypt, and in the Red sea, and in the wilderness forty years” — the witnesses mark the works of the deliverer.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the wonders. Barnes lists the plagues, the dividing of the sea, and the forty years’ guidance. Ellicott joins “wonders and signs” (Deuteronomy 6:22). The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke presses the parallel: “the very person whom they had rejected… was the person alone by whom they were redeemed” — does not Stephen plainly say the same of Christ?

The verse recounts the mighty works by which Moses led Israel out. By wonders and signs in Egypt, at the Red Sea, and through forty years in the wilderness, the rejected deliverer accomplished the redemption of his people. God’s power attended the man the people had refused.

So the witnesses see Stephen press the parallel further: the rejected Moses was vindicated by mighty works, even as Jesus was “approved of God” by miracles and signs. The witnesses note Clarke’s pointed application — the very one Israel had spurned proved to be their only redeemer, attested by wonders in Egypt, at the sea, and in the desert; and Stephen means his hearers to see that the same is true of the Christ they have rejected. They mark the sweep of the deliverer’s works, from the plagues to the long wilderness care, all the fruit of God’s power resting on his chosen instrument. The verse thus reinforces the theme: God authenticates the deliverer the people refuse. As Moses’ wonders proved his divine commission, so Jesus’ signs proved his; and the nation that rejected the worker of these signs has set itself, as its fathers did, against the God who sent and attested him.

So the witnesses see Stephen press the parallel further: the rejected Moses was vindicated by mighty works, even as Jesus was “approved of God” by miracles. The witnesses note Clarke’s pointed application — the very one Israel had spurned proved to be their only redeemer, attested by wonders in Egypt, at the sea, and in the desert; and Stephen means his hearers to see the same is true of the Christ they have rejected. As Moses’ wonders proved his divine commission, so Jesus’ signs proved his; the nation has set itself against the God who sent and attested him.
37:
On Acts 7:37 — “This is that Moses, which said unto the children of Israel, A prophet shall the Lord your God raise up unto you of your brethren, like unto me” — the witnesses mark the prophecy of the coming Prophet.

The grammatical-historical commentators make the application plain. Ellicott: “the parallelism… is now distinctly proclaimed… a fulfilment of the prediction of Deuteronomy 18:18.” Robertson: Stephen argues that Moses predicted the Messiah “like himself… who is no other than Jesus, so that these Pharisees are in reality opposing Moses. It was a neat turn.” The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke: “this very Moses… announced that very prophet whom ye have lately put to death.” The Reformed Calvin: Stephen proves “that Christ is the end of the law.”

The verse brings Moses himself forward as witness to Christ. The same Moses they revered foretold a Prophet like himself, whom God would raise up — the Messiah. Moses pointed beyond himself to the One the nation has now rejected.

So the witnesses see Stephen turn Moses’ own prophecy against his accusers. The witnesses note the “neat turn” Robertson admires: Moses, whom the Sanhedrin claimed to honour, foretold a Prophet like himself; and that Prophet is Jesus, whom they have killed — so that in opposing Christ they oppose the very Moses they profess to defend. They mark that this is the same prophecy Peter had cited (Acts 3:22), now pressed by Stephen to show that the law and Moses lead to Christ. The verse thus deepens the argument: the rejected deliverer Moses was not only a type of the Messiah but his herald, pointing forward to a greater Prophet. To reject Jesus is therefore to reject the One Moses foretold; the charge of blaspheming Moses recoils on the accusers, who have spurned the Prophet Moses promised.

So the witnesses see Stephen turn Moses’ own prophecy against his accusers. The witnesses note the “neat turn” Robertson admires: Moses, whom the Sanhedrin claimed to honour, foretold a Prophet like himself; and that Prophet is Jesus, whom they have killed — so that in opposing Christ they oppose the very Moses they profess to defend. This is the same prophecy Peter cited, now pressed to show that the law and Moses lead to Christ. The charge of blaspheming Moses recoils on the accusers, who have spurned the Prophet Moses promised.
38:
On Acts 7:38 — “This is he, that was in the church in the wilderness with the angel which spake to him in the mount Sina, and with our fathers: who received the lively oracles to give unto us” — the witnesses mark Moses the mediator of the law.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the terms. Robertson: “church in the wilderness” is better “congregation,” the whole nation at Sinai; Moses “received the law from an angel… and so was a mediator” (Galatians 3:19). Clarke: he was “mediator between the angel of God and them”; the “living oracles” are “the doctrines of life.” Wesley: the oracles “chiefly” the ten commandments, “termed living” because God’s word gives life.

The verse honours Moses as the mediator who received and gave the law. In the congregation at Sinai, with the angel and the fathers, he received the living oracles to deliver to Israel. The deliverer is also the law-giver’s mediator.

So the witnesses see Stephen exalt Moses’ office even as he prepares to indict the nation. The witnesses note that “church in the wilderness” means the assembled congregation of Israel, and that Moses stood as mediator between God’s angel and the people, receiving the “lively oracles” — the living, life-giving word of the law. Far from despising Moses or the law, Stephen reveres him as the channel of God’s living words. They mark the irony building in the speech: the nation that boasts of this law-giving heritage has, as the next verses show, repeatedly disobeyed the very oracles Moses gave. The verse thus rebuts the charge that Stephen blasphemed Moses: he honours Moses as mediator of the living law — and so makes the more damning the indictment to come, that Israel received these oracles yet would not keep them, resisting the God who gave them.

So the witnesses see Stephen exalt Moses’ office even as he prepares to indict the nation. The witnesses note that “church in the wilderness” means the assembled congregation of Israel, and that Moses stood as mediator, receiving the “lively oracles” — the living, life-giving word of the law. Far from despising Moses or the law, Stephen reveres him as the channel of God’s living words. The irony builds: the nation that boasts of this heritage has repeatedly disobeyed the very oracles Moses gave — making the more damning the indictment to come.
39:
On Acts 7:39 — “To whom our fathers would not obey, but thrust him from them, and in their hearts turned back again into Egypt” — the witnesses mark the turn to the nation’s rebellion.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the parallel verb. Robertson: “thrust him from them” (aposanto) is “the very verb used of the man who thrust Moses away” (verse 27); they “turned back” in heart to Egypt. Ellicott: “the people rejected Moses then… as they were rejecting Christ now.” The Reformed Calvin: they “gave themselves rather unto the superstitions of Egypt… horrible, and more than blind fury.”

The verse exposes Israel’s ingratitude. The fathers would not obey Moses but thrust him away and in heart turned back to Egypt. Having been delivered, they longed for their bondage and its idols. The deliverer was refused even after the deliverance.

So the witnesses see Stephen begin the indictment that his history has been building toward. The witnesses note the deliberate echo: the same word for “thrusting away” Moses recurs, marking that Israel’s rejection of him was not a single act but a settled disposition. Even after the wonders of the Exodus, the fathers’ hearts turned back to Egypt, preferring the bondage and idolatry they had left. The witnesses mark Calvin’s horror at the perversity — to crave the customs of the land where they had suffered so cruelly. Ellicott draws the point Stephen presses: as the fathers rejected Moses, so this generation rejects Christ, even after a greater deliverance. The verse thus shifts the speech from honouring Moses to arraigning the nation: their fathers’ rebellion against the deliverer is the pattern now repeated, and the heart that turned back to Egypt is the same that now resists the Holy Ghost.

So the witnesses see Stephen begin the indictment his history has been building toward. The witnesses note the deliberate echo: the same word for “thrusting away” Moses recurs, marking that Israel’s rejection was a settled disposition. Even after the wonders of the Exodus, the fathers’ hearts turned back to Egypt, preferring the bondage and idolatry they had left. The speech shifts from honouring Moses to arraigning the nation: their fathers’ rebellion against the deliverer is the pattern now repeated, and the heart that turned back to Egypt is the same that now resists the Holy Ghost.
40:
On Acts 7:40 — “Saying unto Aaron, Make us gods to go before us: for as for this Moses… we wot not what is become of him” — the witnesses mark the demand for idols.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the ingratitude. Robertson: “gods which shall go before us” (Exodus 32:1), perhaps echoing the pillar that had gone before them; “how quickly they had forgotten.” Ellicott discusses whether the calf broke the first or second commandment. The Reformed Calvin marks it “their filthy and detestable treachery… that they made themselves a calf, that they might worship it instead of God.”

The verse records the people’s descent into idolatry. While Moses was in the mount, they demanded that Aaron make them gods to lead them, dismissing the absent deliverer. The rejection of Moses passes at once into the worship of idols.

So the witnesses see in the demand for idols the depth of Israel’s apostasy. Scarcely had they been delivered when, with Moses barely out of sight, they craved visible gods to go before them — forgetting both the Lord who redeemed them and the pillar that had led them. The witnesses note Robertson’s wonder at how quickly they forgot, and Calvin’s judgement that no fouler treachery could be imagined than to make a calf to worship instead of God. They mark the link between rejecting the deliverer and embracing idolatry: having thrust Moses away in heart, they fall at once into the sin of the golden calf. The verse thus carries forward Stephen’s indictment: the nation’s history is one of rejecting God’s servants and turning to false worship — a pattern that exposes the hollowness of their present zeal for the law and the temple, even as they reject the Christ.

So the witnesses see in the demand for idols the depth of Israel’s apostasy. Scarcely had they been delivered when, with Moses barely out of sight, they craved visible gods to go before them — forgetting both the Lord who redeemed them and the pillar that had led them. They mark Robertson’s wonder at how quickly they forgot, and Calvin’s judgement that no fouler treachery could be imagined. The verse carries forward Stephen’s indictment: the nation’s history is one of rejecting God’s servants and turning to false worship — exposing the hollowness of their present zeal even as they reject the Christ.
41:
On Acts 7:41 — “And they made a calf in those days, and offered sacrifice unto the idol, and rejoiced in the works of their own hands” — the witnesses mark the golden calf.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the act and the word. Robertson: “they made a calf” (emoschopoiesan, a word apparently coined here); Stephen “calls it by the right name” — the idol. Ellicott: “rejoiced” expresses “the joy of a feast.” Gill and Wesley note the calf was made “in imitation of Apis,” the Egyptian god. Calvin asks why the ox above all — because in Egypt they made “the greatest account of an ox.”

The verse completes the scene of apostasy. They made the calf, sacrificed to it, and rejoiced in the work of their own hands. The redeemed people feast before an idol of their own making, an Egyptian god set up in the wilderness.

So the witnesses see the golden calf as the emblem of Israel’s recurring idolatry. The witnesses note that the calf was modelled on the Egyptian Apis, betraying how deeply the hearts that “turned back to Egypt” were still captive to its gods. They mark the bitter irony Stephen exposes: the people “rejoiced in the works of their own hands” — worshipping what they themselves had made, feasting before a lifeless idol. Robertson notes that Stephen bluntly names it “the idol,” refusing any softening. The verse thus stands at the centre of the indictment: the very nation that now charges Stephen with blaspheming the temple has a history of gross idolatry from its earliest days. Their fathers, fresh from redemption, made and worshipped a calf; and Stephen will press that this idolatrous bent, far from being cured, ran on through their history — the people of God repeatedly forsaking him for the works of their own hands.

So the witnesses see the golden calf as the emblem of Israel’s recurring idolatry. The witnesses note that the calf was modelled on the Egyptian Apis, betraying how deeply the hearts that “turned back to Egypt” were still captive to its gods. They mark the bitter irony Stephen exposes: the people “rejoiced in the works of their own hands” — worshipping what they themselves had made. The very nation that now charges Stephen with blaspheming the temple has a history of gross idolatry from its earliest days, a bent that ran on through their history.
42:
On Acts 7:42 — “Then God turned, and gave them up to worship the host of heaven; as it is written in the book of the prophets” — the witnesses mark the judicial abandonment.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the dread judgment. Robertson: “gave them up” (paredoken) is the same word thrice in Romans 1:24–28, “like clods on a coffin,” of God giving the heathen to their lusts. Barnes and Ellicott: the “host of heaven” is sun, moon, and stars, worshipped instead of the Lord of hosts (Amos 5:25). Clarke notes “the book of the prophets” means the twelve minor prophets. The Puritan Henry: this giving-up was “the saddest punishment of all for that sin.”

The verse marks God’s judicial response to the calf. He turned away and gave them up to worse idolatry — the worship of the host of heaven. The first apostasy was punished by abandonment to further apostasy, as the prophet records.

So the witnesses see in “God turned and gave them up” the terrible judgment of being abandoned to one’s sin. The witnesses note the weight of the word Robertson marks — the same God uses in Romans when he gives the heathen over to their lusts; here God gives idolatrous Israel up to the worship of sun, moon, and stars. They mark Henry’s point that this judicial abandonment was the saddest punishment of all: to be left to one’s chosen idolatry. The calf was not the end but the beginning of a descent; the first false worship drew on a worse. The verse thus deepens the indictment with a solemn theology of judgment: persistent rejection of God meets not always with immediate stroke but with the dread sentence of being given over. Israel’s history, Stephen shows, is one of idolatry punished by deeper idolatry — a warning to a nation that, resisting the Spirit still, courts the same abandonment.

So the witnesses see in “God turned and gave them up” the terrible judgment of being abandoned to one’s sin. The witnesses note the weight of the word Robertson marks — the same God uses in Romans when he gives the heathen over to their lusts; here God gives idolatrous Israel up to the worship of sun, moon, and stars. They mark Henry’s point that this judicial abandonment was the saddest punishment of all. Israel’s history is one of idolatry punished by deeper idolatry — a warning to a nation that, resisting the Spirit still, courts the same abandonment.
43:
On Acts 7:43 — “Yea, ye took up the tabernacle of Moloch, and the star of your god Remphan, figures which ye made to worship them: and I will carry you away beyond Babylon” — the witnesses mark the depth of idolatry and its sentence.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the idols. Robertson: “the tabernacle of Moloch,” the Ammonite god to whom children were burned. Clarke notes the quotation follows the Septuagint of Amos 5:26, the Hebrew naming Chiun. Wesley: Moloch was the planet Mars, Remphan (Saturn) “represented by a star.” Ellicott discusses the unusual word for “tabernacle.”

The verse names the idols Israel carried and the captivity that judged them. They bore the shrine of Moloch and the star of Remphan, idols of their own making — and for this God would carry them away beyond Babylon. Idolatry led to exile.

So the witnesses see Stephen complete his survey of Israel’s idolatry with its gravest forms and its dread consequence. The witnesses note that the people carried the shrine of Moloch — a god of child-sacrifice — and the star-image of Remphan, even as they bore the ark; a portable idolatry alongside the true worship. They mark that Stephen quotes Amos to show that this was no slander but the testimony of their own prophets, and that the appointed end was exile “beyond Babylon.” The verse thus seals the indictment of idolatry: from the calf to the host of heaven to Moloch and Remphan, the nation’s history was steeped in false worship, and brought down the judgment of captivity. Stephen’s point presses on his hearers: a people with such a record of forsaking God for idols has no ground to charge him with blasphemy, and stands now, in rejecting the Christ, in the same line of resistance that led their fathers into exile.

So the witnesses see Stephen complete his survey of Israel’s idolatry with its gravest forms and dread consequence. The witnesses note that the people carried the shrine of Moloch — a god of child-sacrifice — and the star-image of Remphan, even as they bore the ark; a portable idolatry alongside the true worship. Stephen quotes Amos to show this was no slander but the testimony of their own prophets, and that the appointed end was exile. A people with such a record of forsaking God for idols has no ground to charge him with blasphemy, and stands now, in rejecting Christ, in the same line of resistance.
44:
On Acts 7:44 — “Our fathers had the tabernacle of witness in the wilderness, as he had appointed… according to the fashion that he had seen” — the witnesses mark the true tabernacle, divinely ordered.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the name and the pattern. Robertson: “the tabernacle of the testimony” (marturiou), the tent in the wilderness, not the temple, suggested by contrast with “the tabernacle of Moloch.” Barnes: it was called “of witness” as the visible token of God’s presence. Clarke: it housed the two tables, “a testimony that he had delivered these laws.” The Reformed Calvin: God “had commanded how he would be worshipped,” so the blame for idolatry lies on the people, not on God.

The verse turns from idolatry to the true worship God ordained. Israel had, in the wilderness, the tabernacle of witness, made exactly to the pattern God showed Moses. Over against the idols of their own devising stood the sanctuary of God’s own appointing.

So the witnesses see Stephen set the true, God-given tabernacle against the false worship just condemned. The witnesses note the contrast he draws: while the fathers carried the shrine of Moloch, they also had the “tabernacle of witness” — the sanctuary God himself appointed, built to the heavenly pattern shown Moses, bearing the testimony of the law. They mark Calvin’s point that since God had precisely commanded how he would be worshipped, the guilt of Israel’s idolatry was wholly their own. The verse thus begins the final movement of the speech, leading from the tabernacle to the temple. Stephen honours the divinely-ordained sanctuary even as he prepares to show that God is not confined to it. The true worship was always God’s gift and appointment, not a human invention — and the people’s sin lay in forsaking it for idols, not, as they charged Stephen, in any disrespect to the holy things of God.

So the witnesses see Stephen set the true, God-given tabernacle against the false worship just condemned. The witnesses note the contrast he draws: while the fathers carried the shrine of Moloch, they also had the “tabernacle of witness” — the sanctuary God himself appointed, built to the heavenly pattern, bearing the testimony of the law. They mark Calvin’s point that since God had precisely commanded how he would be worshipped, the guilt of Israel’s idolatry was wholly their own. Stephen honours the divinely-ordained sanctuary even as he prepares to show that God is not confined to it.
45:
On Acts 7:45 — “Which also our fathers that came after brought in with Jesus into the possession of the Gentiles… unto the days of David” — the witnesses mark the tabernacle carried into Canaan.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the name. Clarke and Ellicott: “with Jesus” is the Greek form of “Joshua” (Hebrews 4:8); better rendered Joshua to avoid confusion. Robertson: “in their turn” (diadexamenoi), receiving in succession. Wesley draws two lessons Stephen intimates: “God always loved good men in every land,” and his favour is not bound to one land or temple.

The verse traces the tabernacle from the wilderness into the promised land. The next generation, under Joshua, brought it into the territory of the dispossessed Gentiles, where it remained until David. The movable sanctuary went with the people through their history.

So the witnesses see Stephen continue the history of the true sanctuary toward its settlement in Canaan. The witnesses note the point about the name — “Jesus” here is Joshua, the leader who brought the tabernacle into the land — and that the sanctuary remained a movable tent, carried from place to place, until David’s day. They mark Wesley’s observation of what Stephen quietly intimates: that God’s presence was never tied to a single spot, the tabernacle journeying with his people; and that his favour rests on faithful men in every land. The verse thus advances the argument toward its climax: the tabernacle was always portable, never fixed, and the land itself was given by God’s grace, not held by right. Stephen is building the case that God is not confined to the temple in Jerusalem — a truth that will issue, in the verses following, in the bold declaration that the Most High dwells not in temples made with hands.

So the witnesses see Stephen continue the history of the true sanctuary toward its settlement in Canaan. The witnesses note the point about the name — “Jesus” here is Joshua, the leader who brought the tabernacle into the land — and that the sanctuary remained a movable tent until David’s day. They mark Wesley’s observation of what Stephen quietly intimates: that God’s presence was never tied to a single spot, and his favour rests on faithful men in every land. The argument builds toward its climax: God is not confined to the temple in Jerusalem.
46:
On Acts 7:46 — “Who found favour before God, and desired to find a tabernacle for the God of Jacob” — the witnesses mark David’s desire to build.

The grammatical-historical commentators note David’s petition. Robertson: he “asked for himself” (etesato) a more permanent abode (2 Samuel 7:2; Psalm 132:5). Wesley: “he did not obtain his petition; for God remained without any temple till Solomon built him a house.” Ellicott marks the “indirect answer to the accusation”: Stephen acknowledges the temple was planned by “the man after God’s own heart.”

The verse brings the history to David, who found favour with God and longed to build him a house. The man after God’s heart desired a fixed dwelling for the God of Jacob — a desire God approved but did not grant to David himself.

So the witnesses see Stephen approach the temple with due honour for David’s pious wish. The witnesses note that Stephen, far from despising the temple, traces its origin to David, who “found favour before God” and desired to build a house for the God of Jacob — thus answering, Ellicott observes, the charge of blaspheming the holy place. They mark Wesley’s careful point that David’s petition was not granted to him; God was content to dwell in the tent until Solomon’s day. The verse thus shows the temple as the fruit of devout desire, yet also reminds that for long ages God had no fixed house and was none the less present with his people. Stephen honours David’s longing while quietly preparing the qualification to come: that however glorious the temple David purposed and Solomon built, the Most High is not contained in it — for he dwells not in temples made with hands.

So the witnesses see Stephen approach the temple with due honour for David’s pious wish. The witnesses note that Stephen, far from despising the temple, traces its origin to David, who “found favour before God” and desired to build a house for the God of Jacob — thus answering the charge of blaspheming the holy place. They mark Wesley’s careful point that David’s petition was not granted to him; God was content to dwell in the tent until Solomon’s day. Stephen honours David’s longing while preparing the qualification to come: that however glorious the temple, the Most High is not contained in it.
47:
On Acts 7:47 — “But Solomon built him an house” — the witnesses mark the building of the temple.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Barnes notes David was not permitted to build, being “a man of war” (1 Chronicles 22:8); he prepared the materials, and Solomon built (1 Kings 8:27). The Reformed Calvin observes that Stephen “seemeth… indirectly to rebuke Solomon” — though Solomon built “not without the commandment of God,” with a promise added.

The verse records that Solomon, not David, built the temple. The desire of the father was fulfilled by the son; the fixed house for God was at last erected. Yet, as the next verse will press, even this glorious building could not contain the Most High.

So the witnesses see Stephen reach the temple itself, built by Solomon. The witnesses note that David, the man of war, was not permitted to build, but prepared the way; the actual building fell to Solomon his son. They mark Calvin’s observation that Stephen’s words carry a gentle, indirect rebuke — not of Solomon’s obedience, for he built by God’s command and promise, but of any notion that God could be housed in a building. The verse thus brings the historical survey to its destination: the temple, the holy place Stephen was charged with blaspheming, traced honourably to Solomon’s hand. Stephen grants it all — the favour of David, the building of Solomon — only to set against it the great truth that follows. The temple was real and God-given, but it was never meant to confine the infinite God; and it is this qualification, not any contempt for the holy place, that Stephen is about to declare.

So the witnesses see Stephen reach the temple itself, built by Solomon. The witnesses note that David, the man of war, was not permitted to build, but prepared the way; the actual building fell to Solomon his son. They mark Calvin’s observation that Stephen’s words carry a gentle, indirect rebuke — not of Solomon’s obedience, for he built by God’s command, but of any notion that God could be housed in a building. Stephen grants it all — the favour of David, the building of Solomon — only to set against it the great truth that the temple was never meant to confine the infinite God.
48:
On Acts 7:48 — “Howbeit the most High dwelleth not in temples made with hands; as saith the prophet” — the witnesses mark the great qualification.

The grammatical-historical commentators note its force. Robertson: the emphatic “not” — “the presence of the Most High is not confined in any building… as Solomon himself foresaw” (1 Kings 8:27). Clarke: both Solomon and Stephen mean that “the majesty of” God cannot be contained. Ellicott marks the impression it made: it “broke down the thought of any exclusive holiness in the Temple.” Barnes: “heaven is his throne; the universe his dwelling-place.”

The verse states the truth toward which the whole history has moved. The Most High does not dwell in houses built by men. The temple, real and God-given, could never contain the infinite God — as the prophet, and Solomon himself, declared.

So the witnesses see Stephen reach the climax of his argument about the temple. The witnesses note that this is no novelty or blasphemy but the very truth Solomon owned at the temple’s dedication — that the heaven of heavens cannot contain God, much less a house of hands. Stephen does not despise the temple; he denies only that it confines the Most High. The witnesses mark Ellicott’s observation that this word broke down the notion of an exclusive holiness in the temple, and so placed its eventual downfall within the order of God’s purpose. The verse thus answers the charge against Stephen at its root: he had not blasphemed the holy place, but proclaimed the higher truth that God is greater than any building. The Spirit who hallowed a bush in the desert and dwelt in a movable tent is not imprisoned in Solomon’s temple — a truth the prophet himself confirms in the verses that follow.

So the witnesses see Stephen reach the climax of his argument about the temple. The witnesses note that this is no novelty or blasphemy but the very truth Solomon owned at the dedication — that the heaven of heavens cannot contain God, much less a house of hands. Stephen does not despise the temple; he denies only that it confines the Most High. They mark Ellicott’s observation that this word broke down the notion of an exclusive holiness in the temple, placing its downfall within God’s purpose. The Spirit who hallowed a bush and dwelt in a tent is not imprisoned in Solomon’s temple.
49:
On Acts 7:49 — “Heaven is my throne, and earth is my footstool: what house will ye build me? saith the Lord: or what is the place of my rest?” — the witnesses mark the prophet’s testimony.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the quotation from Isaiah 66:1. Barnes: “what house can be large or magnificent enough for the dwelling of Him who made all things?” Robertson: “what manner of house” carries a scornful force — God “is a spirit and can be worshipped anywhere.” The Reformed Calvin: God “cannot be comprehended within any spaces of place,” so those err who measure his worship “according to their own” conceptions.

The verse cites Isaiah to confirm the truth. Heaven is God’s throne and earth his footstool; what house, then, can men build for him? The God who fills heaven and earth cannot be lodged in a temple of stone.

So the witnesses see Stephen seal his argument with the word of the prophet. The witnesses note that Isaiah’s majestic vision — God enthroned in heaven, the earth his footstool — reduces the proudest temple to insignificance: what house could such a God require? They mark Robertson’s point that the question carries a note of scorn for any thought of confining God, who is spirit and may be worshipped anywhere; and Calvin’s, that God cannot be measured by human notions of place. The verse thus grounds Stephen’s teaching in Scripture the Sanhedrin revered: it is Isaiah, not Stephen, who declares that no house can contain the Lord. The temple, glorious as it was, was never God’s necessary dwelling but a gracious condescension; and to make it an idol, confining God within its walls, is to forget the prophet’s witness to the boundless majesty of the Most High.

So the witnesses see Stephen seal his argument with the word of the prophet. The witnesses note that Isaiah’s majestic vision — God enthroned in heaven, the earth his footstool — reduces the proudest temple to insignificance: what house could such a God require? They mark Robertson’s point that the question carries scorn for any thought of confining God, who is spirit and may be worshipped anywhere. It is Isaiah, not Stephen, who declares that no house can contain the Lord; to make the temple an idol, confining God within its walls, is to forget the prophet’s witness to the boundless majesty of the Most High.
50:
On Acts 7:50 — “Hath not my hand made all these things?” — the witnesses mark the close of the quotation.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Gill notes that in Isaiah the words affirm that God’s hand has made all things, so “what house can be built for him?” Clarke observes that Stephen “had not finished his discourse… but… they immediately raised up a clamour against him.” The Reformed Calvin: God “hath no need… of the sacrifices… his true worship is not contained in ceremonies.”

The verse completes Isaiah’s argument. God’s own hand made heaven and earth and all things; what, then, can men build or offer that he needs? The Creator of all cannot be enriched or housed by the works of his creatures.

So the witnesses see Stephen finish his scriptural proof with the decisive question: has not God made all things? The witnesses note the force of it — the God who created heaven and earth needs no house, no offering, from the hands he himself has made; his true worship cannot be confined to ceremonies or buildings. They mark Calvin’s point that God desires not our gifts for his own sake, but our hearts. The verse thus brings the long historical argument to its theological summit, just before the speech is broken off: the Most High, maker of all, dwells not in temples and needs nothing from men. The witnesses observe Clarke’s suggestion that it was at or near this point, as Stephen’s conclusions told against their idolised temple, that his hearers’ rising fury cut him short — turning the speech, in the next verse, from patient history to sharp rebuke.

So the witnesses see Stephen finish his scriptural proof with the decisive question: has not God made all things? The witnesses note the force of it — the God who created heaven and earth needs no house, no offering, from the hands he himself has made; his true worship cannot be confined to ceremonies or buildings. They mark Calvin’s point that God desires not our gifts for his own sake, but our hearts. The Most High, maker of all, dwells not in temples and needs nothing from men. The witnesses observe Clarke’s suggestion that it was near here, as his conclusions told against their idolised temple, that fury cut Stephen short.
51:
On Acts 7:51 — “Ye stiffnecked and uncircumcised in heart and ears, ye do always resist the Holy Ghost: as your fathers did, so do ye” — the witnesses mark the turn to sharp rebuke.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the change of tone. Robertson: “stiffnecked” (sklerotrachloi) and “uncircumcised” — critics assume Stephen was interrupted, “but the natural climax is sufficient explanation.” Clarke: the metaphor is “from untoward oxen” whose necks cannot be bent to the yoke. Ellicott: “the sudden change… from calm argument to vehement indignation” meets the “uncontrollable” excitement of the Sanhedrin. The Puritan Henry sees the speech moving to show that temple-service must yield to worship in spirit.

The verse turns the patient history into a blazing indictment. Stiffnecked and uncircumcised in heart and ears, the hearers — like their fathers — always resist the Holy Ghost. The survey of the past becomes a charge against the present.

So the witnesses see Stephen, whether interrupted or come to his climax, turn from history to direct accusation. The witnesses note the force of the images: “stiffnecked,” like oxen that will not bend to the yoke; “uncircumcised in heart and ears,” closed to God within though circumcised in flesh. The whole speech has shown a nation that always resisted God’s servants and turned to idols; now Stephen brings the charge home — “as your fathers did, so do ye.” They mark Ellicott’s observation that the change of tone answers the rising fury of the court. The verse thus gathers the indictment to a head: the resistance to the Holy Ghost that marked Israel’s history reaches its climax in this generation, who reject the Christ as their fathers rejected Moses and the prophets. It is the unsparing word of a man filled with the Spirit, naming the nation’s perennial sin to its face — and it seals his own martyrdom.

So the witnesses see Stephen, whether interrupted or come to his climax, turn from history to direct accusation. The witnesses note the force of the images: “stiffnecked,” like oxen that will not bend to the yoke; “uncircumcised in heart and ears,” closed to God within though circumcised in flesh. The whole speech has shown a nation that always resisted God’s servants; now Stephen brings the charge home — “as your fathers did, so do ye.” The resistance to the Holy Ghost that marked Israel’s history reaches its climax in this generation, who reject the Christ as their fathers rejected Moses and the prophets.
52:
On Acts 7:52 — “Which of the prophets have not your fathers persecuted?… of whom ye have been now the betrayers and murderers” — the witnesses mark the charge of killing the prophets and the Just One.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the echo of Christ. Robertson: Jesus had charged them with this very thing (Matthew 23:29–37); the prophets “who foretold the coming of the Messiah their fathers killed.” Ellicott: Stephen “echoes… our Lord’s own words” (Luke 13:34); every witness for truth had to suffer. Barnes: it was “the national characteristic” to persecute God’s messengers.

The verse drives the indictment to its sharpest point. The fathers persecuted and slew the prophets who foretold the Just One; and this generation has become his betrayers and murderers. The pattern of killing God’s messengers culminates in the murder of the Messiah.

So the witnesses see Stephen press the charge to its terrible climax: the nation that killed the prophets has now killed the Christ they foretold. The witnesses note that Stephen echoes the Lord’s own indictment of those who build the prophets’ tombs while sharing their fathers’ murderous spirit. The prophets had announced the coming of the Just One; their fathers slew the announcers; and this generation has crowned the long record by betraying and murdering the One announced. They mark the force of “the Just One” — the Righteous, the sinless Messiah — whose death lies at their door. The verse thus completes the parallel of the whole speech: as Israel rejected Moses and slew the prophets, so they have killed the Christ. Stephen, filled with the Spirit, lays the guilt of the Messiah’s blood squarely on his judges — the unsparing truth that will provoke their murderous rage and make him, in turn, a martyr in the prophets’ line.

So the witnesses see Stephen press the charge to its terrible climax: the nation that killed the prophets has now killed the Christ they foretold. The witnesses note that Stephen echoes the Lord’s own indictment of those who build the prophets’ tombs while sharing their fathers’ murderous spirit. They mark the force of “the Just One” — the Righteous, the sinless Messiah — whose death lies at their door. As Israel rejected Moses and slew the prophets, so they have killed the Christ. Stephen, filled with the Spirit, lays the guilt of the Messiah’s blood squarely on his judges.
53:
On Acts 7:53 — “Who have received the law by the disposition of angels, and have not kept it” — the witnesses mark the final charge of disobedience.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the law and the angels. Robertson and Ellicott: “as ordained of angels” (eis diatagas angelon), the current Jewish belief that angels mediated the giving of the law (Galatians 3:19). Clarke: ranks of angels attended the Divine Majesty, adding grandeur to the giving. Barnes discusses the disputed phrase. The Reformed Calvin marks the irony — they raged against Stephen as a forsaker of the law, while they themselves had not kept it.

The verse delivers the last and sharpest stroke of the indictment. They received the law amid the glory of angels — and have not kept it. The boast of their heritage becomes the measure of their guilt: privileged with the law, they have broken it.

So the witnesses see Stephen close his speech by turning the charge of lawlessness back on his accusers. The witnesses note the bitter justice of it: the Sanhedrin had arraigned Stephen for speaking against the law, yet they, who received it amid the splendour of attending angels, have not kept it — crowning their disobedience by murdering the Just One it foretold. They mark the Jewish tradition, reflected here, that the law was given through angelic mediation, lending it the greater solemnity and their breach the greater guilt. Calvin notes the irony that they called their fury against Stephen “zeal for the law” while themselves transgressing it. The verse thus ends the speech as it climaxed — not with self-defence but with accusation: the true despisers of the law are the judges, not the accused. It is the final provocation that, in the next verse, bursts the Sanhedrin’s restraint into murderous rage.

The witnesses note the bitter justice of it: the Sanhedrin had arraigned Stephen for speaking against the law, yet they, who received it amid the splendour of attending angels, have not kept it — crowning their disobedience by murdering the Just One it foretold. They mark Calvin’s irony that they called their fury “zeal for the law” while themselves transgressing it. The speech ends not with self-defence but with accusation: the true despisers of the law are the judges, not the accused.
54:
On Acts 7:54 — “When they heard these things, they were cut to the heart, and they gnashed on him with their teeth” — the witnesses mark the rage of the Sanhedrin.

The Eastern fathers note the contrast. Chrysostom: “the wrong-doers in trouble… cut to the heart” — yet it was Stephen, “having done no wrong,” who had right to be incensed. The grammatical-historical commentators mark the word. Robertson and Clarke: “cut to the heart” (dieprionto, “sawn through”), a keener pang than the “pricked” of Acts 2:37, producing “not repentance, but the frenzy of furious anger.” Ellicott notes the gnashing teeth as the only literal use of the phrase in the New Testament.

The verse records the fury Stephen’s words provoked. The Sanhedrin are cut to the heart and gnash their teeth in rage. The same truth that pierced three thousand to repentance at Pentecost here pierces the judges to murderous fury.

So the witnesses see the dread contrast between conviction unto life and conviction unto wrath. The witnesses note the very word: Stephen’s speech “sawed through” their hearts, a deeper cut than Peter’s at Pentecost — but where that wound led to the cry “what shall we do?”, this leads to gnashing teeth and the thirst for blood. The same sword of the word that breaks some hearts to repentance hardens others to fury. They mark Chrysostom’s observation of the reversal — the guilty are in torment, while the innocent man stands calm. The verse thus shows the parting of the ways that the gospel always works: confronted with the truth, the heart is either humbled or enraged. The judges, refusing to repent, are mastered by their wrath; and their gnashing teeth foretell the violence that, in the verses following, will fall on the Spirit-filled martyr who stands serene before them.

So the witnesses see the dread contrast between conviction unto life and conviction unto wrath. The witnesses note the very word: Stephen’s speech “sawed through” their hearts, a deeper cut than Peter’s at Pentecost — but where that wound led to “what shall we do?”, this leads to gnashing teeth and the thirst for blood. The same sword of the word that breaks some hearts to repentance hardens others to fury. They mark Chrysostom’s observation of the reversal — the guilty in torment, the innocent man calm. Refusing to repent, the judges are mastered by their wrath.
55:
On Acts 7:55 — “But he, being full of the Holy Ghost, looked up stedfastly into heaven, and saw the glory of God, and Jesus standing on the right hand of God” — the witnesses mark the martyr’s vision.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the standing Christ. Robertson: full of the Spirit, gazing up, “he saw God’s glory and Jesus standing as if he had risen to cheer the brave Stephen” — elsewhere he is pictured “sitting” (Acts 2:34). Clarke: “the Shekinah… and Jesus standing… in his official character, as Mediator.” Ellicott marks that this description, full of the Spirit, frames Stephen’s whole work from first to last.

The verse sets the serene glory of the martyr against the fury of his judges. Filled with the Spirit, Stephen looks up and sees the glory of God and Jesus — not seated, but standing — at God’s right hand. Heaven opens to the man earth is about to cast out.

So the witnesses see in Stephen’s vision the comfort and vindication of the dying saint. The witnesses fasten on the wonder that Jesus is seen standing, where Scripture elsewhere pictures him seated at God’s right hand; they read it, with Robertson, as the risen Lord rising to welcome and to cheer his faithful witness — standing to receive him, to honour him, to plead for him. They mark that this vision was granted precisely as the rage of the court reached its height: against the gnashing teeth of men, heaven opens and the glory of God shines on the martyr. Filled with the Spirit from first to last, Stephen sees what his enemies cannot — the exalted Christ. The verse thus shows the sustaining grace given in the hour of death: the same Spirit that emboldened his speech now opens his eyes to glory, so that he meets his murderers not with terror but with the sight of his Lord standing to receive him.

So the witnesses see in Stephen’s vision the comfort and vindication of the dying saint. The witnesses fasten on the wonder that Jesus is seen standing, where Scripture elsewhere pictures him seated; they read it, with Robertson, as the risen Lord rising to welcome and cheer his faithful witness — standing to receive him, to honour him. They mark that this vision was granted precisely as the court’s rage peaked: against the gnashing teeth of men, heaven opens and the glory of God shines. The same Spirit that emboldened his speech now opens his eyes to glory, so he meets his murderers with the sight of his Lord.
56:
On Acts 7:56 — “And said, Behold, I see the heavens opened, and the Son of man standing on the right hand of God” — the witnesses mark the martyr’s confession.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the rare title. Lightfoot: “Christ frequently calls himself the Son of man, but it is rarely that we find him so called by others” — Stephen here recites Daniel 7:13. Robertson: the title is elsewhere “in Christ’s own words,” perhaps recalling Matthew 26:64. Ellicott: “the vision was given to the inward spiritual eye,” no priest seeing it. The Reformed Calvin: God meant “to wring and torment his enemies,” as Stephen “courageously triumph[s]” over them.

The verse gives Stephen’s open confession of what he sees. The heavens are opened, and the Son of man stands at God’s right hand. He proclaims aloud the vision of the exalted Christ — the very claim for which Jesus had been condemned.

So the witnesses see Stephen, in the face of death, confess the exalted Christ in the words of Daniel’s prophecy. The witnesses note the striking title “Son of man” — rarely on any lips but Christ’s own — by which Stephen identifies the One he sees with the heavenly figure of Daniel, coming in glory; and they recall that it was for claiming this very dignity, the Son of man at God’s right hand, that Jesus was condemned by this same council. Stephen’s confession thus seals the parallel: he proclaims the truth for which his Lord died. They mark Calvin’s point that the vision both comforted Stephen and tormented his enemies, who heard him triumph over their judgment. The verse shows the martyr’s courage at its height: not silenced by their fury, he openly declares the glorified Christ — a confession the Sanhedrin can only hear as blasphemy, and which provokes the final, fatal violence.

So the witnesses see Stephen, in the face of death, confess the exalted Christ in the words of Daniel’s prophecy. The witnesses note the striking title “Son of man” — rarely on any lips but Christ’s — by which Stephen identifies the One he sees with Daniel’s heavenly figure; and they recall that it was for claiming this very dignity that Jesus was condemned by this same council. Stephen’s confession seals the parallel: he proclaims the truth for which his Lord died. They mark Calvin’s point that the vision both comforted Stephen and tormented his enemies, who heard him triumph over their judgment.
57:
On Acts 7:57 — “Then they cried out with a loud voice, and stopped their ears, and ran upon him with one accord” — the witnesses mark the outburst of violence.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the frenzy. Robertson: “stopped their ears” (syneschon), holding them with their hands, “affected to believe Stephen guilty of blasphemy” (Matthew 26:65); “rushed upon him with one accord.” Clarke: stopping the ears was “a proof that he had uttered blasphemy.” Ellicott marks the contrast with the forms of fair trial observed at Christ’s condemnation — “now all restraint was removed, and fanaticism had full play.”

The verse records the eruption of mob fury. Crying out, stopping their ears against the “blasphemy,” the council and crowd rush upon Stephen with one accord. Reason and order collapse into violence.

So the witnesses see the trial dissolve into a lynching. The witnesses note the gestures of feigned piety masking murderous rage: they stop their ears as if unable to bear blasphemy, even as they prepare to commit murder. The confession of the exalted Christ, which to Stephen was glory, is to them intolerable; and, all pretence of justice abandoned, they rush on him in a body. They mark Ellicott’s observation that where Christ’s condemnation kept some forms of trial, here fanaticism has full play. The verse thus shows the final hardening of those “cut to the heart” but unrepentant: refusing the truth, they silence the witness by force. The stopped ears are the emblem of a willful deafness to God’s word, and the united rush the proof that conviction unrepented turns to murderous hatred — the violence that will make Stephen the first martyr of the Church.

58:
On Acts 7:58 — “And cast him out of the city, and stoned him: and the witnesses laid down their clothes at a young man’s feet, whose name was Saul” — the witnesses mark the stoning and the first sight of Saul.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the procedure and the name. Barnes and Lightfoot: the stoning was “without the city” (Leviticus 24:14), the witnesses required to cast the first stones. Ellicott: “were stoning him” (imperfect), the shower going on through his prayers. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley addresses Saul directly: “O Saul, couldst thou have believed… that thou thyself shouldst be stoned in the same cause?… His dying prayer reached thee.”

The verse records the martyrdom and introduces Saul. They cast Stephen out and stoned him, the witnesses laying their garments at the feet of a young man named Saul. The first martyr dies; and the future apostle stands by, consenting.

So the witnesses see the execution carried out and mark the providence in the bystander. The witnesses note that even this mob violence kept some legal form — the stoning outside the city, the witnesses casting the first stones, as the law prescribed for a blasphemer — though the trial itself was a travesty. They fasten on the quiet but momentous detail: the clothes laid at the feet of Saul, who guarded them in approval of the deed. Wesley’s apostrophe captures the wonder — that this consenting young man would himself one day suffer for the same Christ, his conversion perhaps owing something to the dying martyr’s prayer. The verse thus joins the death of the first witness to the first appearance of the great apostle to the Gentiles, and hints at the mysterious workings of grace: the blood of the martyr and the consent of the persecutor are woven together in God’s purpose, which will turn Saul the persecutor into Paul the witness.

59:
On Acts 7:59 — “And they stoned Stephen, calling upon God, and saying, Lord Jesus, receive my spirit” — the witnesses mark the martyr’s prayer to Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators note a textual point of great weight. Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson: the word “God” is not in the Greek; it reads “they stoned Stephen, invoking and saying, Lord Jesus, receive my spirit” — direct prayer to Jesus. Ellicott marks it “an instance of direct prayer addressed… to Christ as God,” recalling Pliny’s report of Christian worship. Wesley: “such a solemn prayer to Christ… no good man could have paid” were he not God.

The verse records Stephen’s dying invocation. As the stones fall, he prays — not to God in general, but to the Lord Jesus — “receive my spirit.” The martyr commits his soul into the hands of the Christ he has just seen standing in glory.

So the witnesses fasten on the dying prayer as a testimony to the deity of Christ. The witnesses note the textual truth that “God” is no part of the original: Stephen prays directly to the Lord Jesus, committing his spirit to him as Jesus had committed his to the Father. They mark the doctrinal weight Ellicott and Wesley press — that such a prayer, the surrender of a departing soul, is an act of worship that none but God may receive; so the first martyr’s dying breath confesses the Godhead of Christ. They observe the fittingness: having just seen the Son of man standing to receive him, Stephen asks that Lord to receive his spirit. The verse thus crowns the martyrdom with the highest confession: the Jesus condemned by this council as a blasphemer is, to his dying witness, the Lord to whom prayer is made and into whose hands the soul is committed — the very deity the Sanhedrin denied, now sealed by a martyr’s blood.

60:
On Acts 7:60 — “And he kneeled down, and cried with a loud voice, Lord, lay not this sin to their charge. And when he had said this, he fell asleep” — the witnesses mark the martyr’s forgiveness and death.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the likeness to Christ. Barnes and Ellicott: the prayer “strikingly resembles the dying prayer of the Lord Jesus” (Luke 23:34) — evidence that Luke’s account of the crucifixion was known to Stephen. Robertson: “kneeled down”; Lightfoot: “fell asleep,” a common term for death. The Reformed Calvin: Stephen “joineth the love of men with faith in Christ”; we must put on this affection if we would be gathered to Christ.

The verse closes the martyrdom with a prayer of forgiveness and the peace of death. Kneeling, Stephen cries aloud for his murderers’ pardon — and then “falls asleep.” His last words are mercy; his death, a quiet rest.

So the witnesses see the first martyr die in the very spirit of his Lord. The witnesses note the unmistakable echo of Christ on the cross: as Jesus prayed “Father, forgive them,” so Stephen kneels and cries “Lord, lay not this sin to their charge” — his last act a prayer for his killers, his enemies’ pardon his dying concern. They mark Calvin’s point that true faith in Christ joins with love even for deadly foes, and that this affection must mark all who would be gathered to Christ. They observe the tender word for his death — “he fell asleep” — the violence of stoning transmuted, for the believer, into a peaceful rest in Jesus. The verse thus completes the portrait of Stephen as the faithful witness conformed to his Master: filled with the Spirit, beholding the glory, praying to the Lord Jesus, and forgiving his murderers. His blood, falling at the feet of Saul, becomes the seed of the Church, and his death the pattern of Christian martyrdom — triumphant, forgiving, and at peace.
